q:
SECTION A: THEMES FROM THE OLD TESTAMENT

 

(a) Highlight the story of how the Israelites crossed the Red Sea.

(b) State two lessons which Christians can learn from this incident
explanation:
HOW THE ISRAELITES CROSSED THE RED SEA. (EXODUS 14).

i. After the ten plagues, Pharaoh allowed the Israelites to leave Egypt for the wilderness to worship their God.

ii. Later, when Pharaoh realized that the Israelites had left for good, he regretted for allowing them to go.

iii The Israelites were guarded by the Lord by a pillar of cloud during the day and pillar of fire by night.

iv. Pharaoh quickly organized his soldiers and chariots to chase the Israelites with the hope of bringing them back.

v. God allowed Pharaoh and his charioteers to pursue the Israelites in order to gain glory over the Egyptians.

vi. They caught up with them at Pihahiroth where they had encamped in front of the sea.

vii. When the Egyptians drew nearer, the Israelites became afraid and panicked/displaying lack of faith in God and Moses.

vii. They cried to the Lord and began to accuse Moses for bringing them into the wilderness to die.

ix. They said that they should have been left in Egypt to serve the Egyptians than to die in the wilderness.

x. Moses told them not to entertain any fear but to stand firm for the Lord would fight for them.

xi. Moses again said that in no time they would not see the Egyptians again.

xii. Moses then cried to God and he was told by God to stretch his rod over the sea.

xii. After that, a great wind Came and drove back the waters.

xiv. The Israelites passed through on dry land/ground.

xv. The Egyptians gave them a hot chase, but the wheels of their chariots could not move

xvi. Moses immediately stretched his rod over the sea and the waters returned leading to the drowning of the Egyptians.

xvii. The people then came to believe in Yahweh and in Moses His servant.

 

B. LESSONS TO BE LEARNT FROM THE CROSSING OF THE RED SEA

İ. Need to have faith in God.

ii. God is always ready to protect his people in time of crisis/ provide a Way of escape for his people.

iii. God will never allow our enemies to overcome us.

iv. God is faithful and reliable/ always loyal to his people.

V. Need to be patient in times of trouble/ crisis. vi. Need to pray always in difficult times

vii. Need to seek God at all times/ being obedient to God's word/command.

vii. We should have trust in our leaders.

ix. Leaders must be Confident always and inspire their followers to have same.

x. Leaders should encourage their followers always in time of difficulty to stand firm and not panic.

xi. God punishes those who do not believe in Him/humbles them.

xii. God is sovereign/Can do the impossible.

xiii. Fear and panic weaken our faith and cause us to speak evil against God and spiritual leaders

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q:
SECTION A: THEMES FROM THE OLD TESTAMENT

 

(a) However, I will not tear away all the kingdom, but I will give one tribe to your son, for the sake David my servant.....Recount what led to the above pronouncement
explanation:
A. REASONS FOR THE PRONOUNCEMENT (1 KINGS 11:1-13)

 

i. When Solomon had established himself as King of lsrael after his father David, he started loving many foreign women of whom God had warned him not to marry.

ii. Those women included the daughter of Pharaoh and Moabite, Edomite, Sidonian and Hittite Women.

iii. The reason was that they would surely turn away his heart after their gods.

iv. Thus, during the latter part of Solomon's reign, his heart turned away from God who had appeared to him twice.

V. This came about because he married many foreign wives against God's will.

vi, He had seven hundred wives and three hundred concubines and these women-led him astray.

vii. Solomon built high places for the gods of his foreign wives who burnt incense and sacrificed to their gods there,

viii, He built high places for Chemosh, the abomination of Moab, Molech the abomination. of Ammon,

ix, God was angry with Solomon because his heart had turned away from the God of Israel.

x. His marriage of foreign wives also led to the adulteration of the worship of Yahweh.

xi. God was not pleased with Solomon, because he did not follow Him wholeheartedly as his father, David did.

xi. God, therefore, decided to divide the Davidic Kingdom of Israel into two after the death of Solomon.

xiii. The Lord said to Solomon "Since this has been your mind and you have not kept my Covenant and status which l have commanded you, I will surely tear the Kíngdom from you and give it to your servant".

xiv. One tribe (Judah) was to be retained for the sake of David and for the sake of Jerusalem which Yahweh had chosen.

Xv. Solomon who had previously been devoted to God backslid from the worship of God/His wives turned away his heart after other gods.

xvi. Solomon was however assured that for the sake of his father David, the prophecy would not happen during his days but during the days of his son.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q:
SECTION A: THEMES FROM THE OLD TESTAMENT

 

(a) Outline Nehemiah's immediate reactions to the news about the state of affairs in Jerusalem

(b) Identify three virtues that can be learnt from Nehemiah's reactions
explanation:
A) HOW NEHEMIAH REACTED TO THE NEWS ABOUT THE STATE OF AFFAIRS IN JERUSALEM (NEHEMIAH 1:2-11)

 

i. Nehemiah was one of the Jews taken into exile by Nebuchadnezzar

ii. He was made a CUp-bearer to the king of Persia in Susa.

iii. One day, Nehemiah was in Susa when a delegation led by his brother Hanani came to him with men from Judah.

iv. Nehemiah asked them concerning the few Jews who were still in Jerusalem and about the city itself.

v. Hanani made him aware that the people in Jerusalem were in great trouble and shame.

vi. He further said that the walls of Jerusalem were broken down and its gates destroyed by fire.

vii. Upon hearing that, Nehemiah broke down, wept, mourned and fasted for days.

viii. Nehemiah prayed to God to hear the prayer of his servants

ix. He confessed that He and his father's house had sinned.

x. Again, he Confessed that they had not kept the commandments, statutes and ordinances Moses commanded.

xi. He asked God to remember His words to Moses that if they were unfaithful he would scatter them among the people.

xii. But should they repent and keep His commandment, He would gather them and restore them to the place He has chosen for them

xiii. He reminded God that He had redeemed His people by His great power.

xiv. He finally petitioned God to listen to His servant and grant him success and mercy.

xv. After the prayer, God intervened and touched the heart of the king to grant him mercy, to visit his people.

 

B. VIRTUES THAT CAN BE LEARNT FROM NEHEMIAH'S REACTION

 

i. Nehemiah was patriotic; hence, we need to learn to show patriotism/ love our nation.

ii. He was very prayerful. We need to emulate Nehemiah and be prayerful.

iii. He was conscious of the sins Committed by him and his fellow Jews; so he confessed to God/ There is the need for a country to accept her sins and confession.

iV. There is the need for national repentance.

V. It is important to have faith in God/God is faithful.

vi. We should always intercede for our nation/ pray for the well-being of our country.

vii. There is the need to be focused/ committed.

vii. We should have vision and share it with others.

ix. We should be sincere to God by confessing our sins.

x. We should show empathy with the plight of our people/selfless.

xi. We should demonstrate humility and sincere service.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q:
SECTION A: THEMES FROM THE OLD TESTAMENT

 

(a) l am no prophet, nor a prophet's son, but I am a herdsman and a dresser of sycamore trees...give an account of the events that led to this statement.

(b) ldentify three reasons for which people complain about the activities of some men of God today.
explanation:
A. AMOS' ENCOUNTER WITH AMAZIAH (AMOS 7:10-17)

 

i. Amos was a shepherd in Judah whom God called to prophesy in Israel.

ii. He spoke against the social and religious malpractices of the Israelites of his day.

iii. Amaziah, the priest at Bethel, reported Amos to the king of Israel, Jeroboam l, accusing him of conspiracy and treason.

iv. According to Amaziah, Amos had prophesied that the king of Israel would go into exile.

v. Amaziah then met Amos who had told him that he was forbidden to speak against the king and the temple.

vi, Amaziah misconstrued Amos for one of the many false prophets of the time and called him a seer (a professional prophet who prophesies for a living).

vi. Amaziah commanded Amos to return to Judah, his home town to prophesy and earn a living there and never again at Bethel.

vii. This was because Bethel was the King's sanctuary and the temple of the kingdom.

ix. Amos disassociated himself from the wrong impression created about him by Amaziah.

x. He told Amaziah that the Lord called him to prophesy in Israel while he was tending his flock and dressing the sycamore trees.

xi. And for challenging him, Amos told Amaziah that his wife would become a harlot in the city and his sons shall fall by the sword and he Amaziah would die in exile.

 

B.WHY PEOPLE COMPLAIN ABOUT THE ACTIVITIES OF SOME MEN OF GOD

 

i. Use of occultic/magical powers.

ii. Commercialisation of the gospel/extortion.

iii. Discrimination against the p0or and underprivileged. 

iv. Immorality/ immoral lives.

v. False doctrines.

vi. False prophecies/ not trustworthy.

vii. Lack of adequate education.

vii. Embezzlement/ misappropriation of funds.

ix. Over-emphasis on prosperity messages above salvation and redemption.

x. Political alignment.

xi. Display of wealth and opulence.

xi. Mismanagement of church resources.

xii. Abuse of power and position.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q:
THEMES FROM THE SYNOPTIC GOSPELS AND THE ACTS OF THE APOSTLES
'

(a) Relate the trial of Jesus before the Sanhedrin according to Mark's gospel.

(b) Give two reasons for which people bear false witness against others.
explanation:
A. THE TRIAL OF JESUS BEFORE THE ŠANHEDRIN ACCORDING TO MARK (MARK 14:53-65)

i. After Jesus was arrested in the garden of Gethsemane, Peter followed at a distance into the courtyard of the high priest and there warmed himself at the fire.

ii. The Sanhedrin sought false testimony against Jesus to put him to death but they found none.

iii. The Sanhedrin was a body of religious leaders presided by the high priest.

iv. Many people came to give false evidence against him but their statements did not tally.

v. But finally, in accordance 1lse with Jewish law, two witnesses came forward and accused Jesus of saying that he would destroy the temple Jon in Jerusalem and in three days build another.

vi. The temple stood as the centre of Judaism; thus, Jesus was attacking the heart of the Jewish religion.

vii. The high priest then asked Jesus under oath whether he was the Messiah, the son of the Blessed One.

vii. Jesus replied affirmatively that he was the Messiah.

ix. He also said that they would see the Son of Man seated at the right hand of power and coming with the clouds of heaven.

x. The high priest then tore his garment as a sign of righteous indignation and asked if they needed a further evidence against this blasphemy.

xi. According to the high priest, Jesus had spoken blasphemy, so, he sought to the decision of the council.

xii. The whole council condemned Jesus and said that he deserved death as a ber penalty for blasphemy.

xiii. Eventually, some members of the Sanhedrin spat on him, while others covered his face and struck him to express their contempt and hate for his Messianic claims.

xiv. They asked Jesus to prophesy and mention the one who struck him.

xv. The guards likewise gave him some blows

 

B. REASONS WHY PEOPLE BEAR FALSE WITNESS AGAINST OTHERS

İ. Hatred/character assassination/ defamation

ii. Envy/ jealousy

iii. Greed

iv. Vengeance/ retribution

v. Bribery/monetary gains

vi. To win favour

vi. Religious/tribal /filial reasons

vii. Coercion/ force

ix. Out of fear

x. For selfish interest

xi. To incriminate eliminate

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q:
THEMES FROM THE SYNOPTIC GOSPELS AND THE ACTS OF THE APOSTLES

(a) My soul is very sorrowful, even to death; remain here and watch with me. Give an account of what happened to Jesus after he made this statement.

(b) identify two ways by which one can assist a fellow Christian going through challenges
explanation:
A. JESUS' AGONY AND ARREST IN THE GARDEN OF GETHSEMANE (MATTHEW 26:36-56)

i. Jesus and his disciples went to the garden of Gethsemane to pray.

ii. He took with him Peter, James and John (member of his inner circle) to another place, leaving the rest behind.

iii. Jesus then left the three and went further to pray alone.

iv. He became greatly distressed and made the above statement.

v. He then went a little farther on and fell tọ the ground to pray, saying my father if it be possible, let this cup pass from me; neverthless God's will should be done

vi. This prayer shows the profound humanity of Jesus and his resolute courage and submission to the will of God.

vii. The cup in this context is a symbol or deep sorrow and suffering.

viii. Jesus came back to the three, Peter, James and John found them sleeping and he asked Peter whether they could not watch with him for one hour.

ix. Jesus then told them to watch and pray so that they would not enter into temptation.

x. He said the spirit is willing but the body is weak.

xi. This means that though the disciples had the best of intentions, the weakness of the flesh failed them.

xii. Again, for the second time, Jesus went away to pray and he again asked that if it was possible the cup should pass by him.

xii. Jesus came back and found the three disciples asleep again

xiv. Jesus left them and went to pray for the third time and he came back to meet the disciples still asleep.

xv. He asked them whether they were still sleeping and taking their rest.

Xvi. Jesus then told them that the hour was at hand and the Son of Man would be betrayed into the hands of sinners.

xvi. He made them aware that the betrayer was at hand so they should get ready to go.

xvii. As Jesus was speaking,. Judas Iscariot led a crowd with swords and clubs to arrest him.

xix. But one of these who stood by drew his sword and cut the ear of the high priest's slave but Jesus restrained him.

xx. The crowd then arrested Jesus after Judas had kissed him.

xxi. They led Him to the high priest's house, for the Jewish trial.

 

B. WAYS BY WHICH ONE GOING THROUGH CHALLENGES CAN BE ASSISTED 

i. Visiting the one going through the challenges.

ii. Praying with the person having the problem.

iii. Assisting the person in cash or in-kind when necessary/ sharing your resources with him or her.

iv. Counselling the victim/ providing advice.

v. Relocating the victim/ changing victim's environment.

vi. Seeking redress/justice.

vii. Speaking on their behalf advocacy.

vii. Encouraging the person to trust in God.

ix. Show love/empathy.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q:
THEMES FROM THE SYNOPTIC GOSPELS AND THE ACTS OF THE APOSTLES

 

(a) Give an account of Stephen's defence before the Sanhedrin.

(b) State three lessons that can be learnt from Stephen's defence.
explanation:
STEPHEN'S DEFENCĚ BEFORE THE SANHEDRIN (ACT6: 2-6; 8-9 7:1-10)

i. Stephen was one of the seven deacons appointed to be in charge of the welfare of the poor and needy in the early church.

ii. He was full of faith, grace and power and performed great wonders and signs among the people.

iii. Some of the Jews argued with Stephen but they could not withstand the wisdom and the spirit with which he spoke.

iv. They instigated some men who accused Stephen of speaking blasphemous words against God and Moses.

v Specifically, that Jesus of Nazareth would destroy the temple, and change the customs which Moses delivered to them.

vi. By Jewish law, the accusations leveled against Stephen constituted serious offence punishable by death.

vii. In his defence, Stephen tried to prove that even in the past, God did not confine His presence to the temple at Jerusalem.

viii. He decided to trace the history of the Jews to show that God is universal and does not only dwell in the temple.

ix. He cited the example of how God appeared to Abraham when he was in Mesopotamia and asked him to go and live in Haran and Abraham obeyed.

x. He stressed that God told Abraham that his descendants would become slaves in another country where they would be maltreated for four hundred years

xi. But God would punish that nation which enslaved them and later Abraham's decendants would go out and Worship him in the land of Canaan.

Xii. Again, Stephen said that God gave Abraham the covenant of circumcision and when he became the father of Isaac, he circumcised him on the eighth day.

xii. Isaac also became the father of Jacob and Jacob became the father of the patriarchs.

xiv. The Patriarchs were jealous of Jacob and sold him into Egypt.

xv. God was with Joseph and rescued him from all afflictions.

 

B.LESSONS TÖ BE LEARNT FROM STEPHEN'S DEFENCE

I. We should be bold and courageous when we face trials.

ii. We should have strong faith in God.

iii. In times of persecution, we should rely on God to defend us 

iv When God blesses us, the enemy cannot overcome us

v There is the need to remember our history

vi bearing false witness destroys one's personality/ integrity

vii To obtain protection against the devil. we should always pray.

viii. We should be prepared to defend our faith even when we face opposition

ix We should forgive those who hurt us.

x. We should be bold to spread the gospel.

xi We should be knowledgeable in scriptures.

xii. We should be respectful to elders.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q:
SECTION C: THEMES FROM SELECTED EPISTLES

(a) Therefore confess your sins to one another, and pray for one another, that you may be healed... What other teachings did James give on effective prayer?

(b) In what three ways are Christians misusing prayers today?
explanation:
A. JAMES' TEACHING ON EFFECTIVE PRAYER(JAMES 1:2-8; 5:13-18)

 

i To James, effective prayer is one that God answers.

ii. And to achieve this, one needs to repent and confess one's sins to God and to one another in order to attract God's healing and forgiveness of sin,

iii James encouraged Christians to count it all joy whenever they meet trials.

iv. He says when one's faith is tested through trials, one becomes steadfast and later becomes perfect and complete.

v. According to James, if anyone lacks wisdom, he should ask God in prayer and he would have it generously.

vi. James holds the view that one must pray in faith without doubting

Vil. For anyone who doubts is like a wave of the sea that is driven and tossed by the wind.

vii. And that, a person who doubts will receive nothing from the Lord.

ix. He again said that if anyone is indisposed, he should call upon the elders of the church to anoint him with oil and pray in humility,.

x. And that, the prayer of faith will heal the sick.

xi. James revealed that God is glorified when people pray for the right things.

xii. Thus, prayer should be made according to the will of God.

xii. James urged Christians to pray in humility.

XiV. Christians should be persistent in prayer by praying unceasingly till their requests are met

xv. James is of the opinion that the fervent prayer of the righteous has a great effect

xvi. He gave an example of Elijah who prayed fervently for no rain and there was no rain for three and half years.

Xvii. And after three and half years, he prayed again and there was rain

 

B. HOW PRAYERS ARE MISUSED BY CHRISTIANS TODAY

İ Paying money for prayers/ consultation fee for prayers/ Commercialisation of prayers.

ii. Cursing people in prayers/ praying against enemies.

iii Praying at the wrong time when they should be working/substituting prayer for work.

iv. Shouting making noise to disturb others.

v. Prayers for the highest donor/ bidders.

vi. Substituting prayers for righteousness.

vii. Praying with selfish motives.

viii. Praying not in accordance with the word/will of God.

ix. idolatry in prayer/praying to religious leaders rather than God.

X. Effectiveness of prayer wrongly associated with material objects, e.g. salt candle, stickers, anointing oil, etc.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q:
SECTION C: THEMES FROM SELECTED EPISTLES

 

(a) Highlight the attitude Peter recommended for Christians living among people of other faiths.

(b) State two factors that can make Christians turn away from pleasing God.
explanation:
A. ATTITUDE PETER RECOMMENDED FOR CHRISTIANS LIVING AMONG PEOPLE OF OTHER FAITHS (1 PETER 2: 1-25).

 

i. Peter advised Christians to put away all malice, deceit, hypocrisy, envy, and slander

ii He said Christians should long for the pure spiritual milk (the word of God) so that they may grow up unto salvation

iii. Peter referred to Christians as a chosen race, a royal priesthood, a holy nation.

iv He further said that they are a people for His own possession who have been called out of darkness into His marvelous light.

v According to Peter, Christians were formerly not a people but now they are God's people, /hitherto they had not received mercy but now they have received mercy

vi, Peter urged Christians that, as sojourners and exiles, they should abstain from the passions of the flesh which wage war against the soul (E.g. Drunkenness/ lawlessness/idolatry

vii Peter said Christians should do good deeds among Gentiles so that they may see their good deeds and give glory to God/maintain good conduct

viii. In his letter, Peter advised Christians to be loyal to their masters whether they are gentle or oppressive / Be subjected to every human institution.

ix, He said Christians should do good so as to silence the ignorance of foolish men.

x. Again, Christians should live as free people of God but they should not use their freedom as cover-up for evil

xi. According to Peter, Christians should live as servants of God and should honor everyone

xii Christians should also love the brotherhood, fear God, and honor the emperor

xiii. Peter says a servant Is approved of his calling if he endured pain while suffering oppression

xiv According to Peter,  it is unacceptable if a servant is beaten for his wrong deed and he retaliates

xv But when one does evil and suffers for it, yet bears it patiently, he wins God's approval.

Xvi. Peter finally said that Jesus is a good example of the suffering servant who should be emulated

 

B, FACTORS THAT CAN MAKE CHRISTIANS TURN AWAY FROM PLEASING GOD

i Desires of the flesh/greed. 

li, False doctrines.

iii Harsh economic conditions

iv. Ignorance of the word of God.

v. Persecutions/ fear of men.

vi. Difficult bitter or unpleasant experiences/ situations.

vii Materialism

vii. Cultural traditional practices

viii Unforgiving spirit.

ix. Lack of faith.

x. Disappointments thinking God is not listening.

xi, Occultic practices

xii Discrimination / Favouritism

In 1 Samuel 15:23, the prophet Samuel delivers a powerful message to King Saul, emphasising that rebellion is as sinful as divination. 

1 Samuel 15:23 (RSV):

“For rebellion is like the sin of divination, and stubbornness is like iniquity and idolatry. Because you have rejected the word of the Lord, he has also rejected you from being king.”

As punishment for causing Israel to sin and God to anger, in the book of 1 Kings 21:22, God said he would make Ahab's house like that of Jeroboam son of Nebat, and that of Baasha son of Ahijah. 

Recall that after Jeroboam's death, he was succeeded by his son Nadab of Israel who only reigned for 2 years before he was assassinated by his eventual successor, Baasha of Israel, a son of Ahijah and a member of the Tribe of Issachar. Baasha proceeded to exterminate all members of the House of Jeroboam. God told Ahab he would suffer a similar fate

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q: Which of the following disciples was called from the tax office by Jesus?
explanation:
Levi and Matthew are actually the same person:

  • In Mark 2:14 and Luke 5:27–29, the man at the tax office is called Levi, son of Alphaeus.

  • In Matthew 9:9, the same event is described, but the tax collector is called Matthew.

From this, Bible scholars generally agree that Levi is another name for Matthew, the disciple who later became one of the twelve apostles and the traditional author of the Gospel of Matthew.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q:
(a) Highlight the steps taken by God to change a formless earth to a place of life and activity
(b) ldentify four ways human beings are destroying God's creation.
explanation:
A. How God changed a formless earth to a place of life and activity (Genesis 1:1-31):

i. In the beginning, God created the heavens and the earth.

ii. The earth was formless, and the spirit of God hovered on the face of the waters.

iii. God commanded the light to appear on the formless, empty, and dark earth.

iv. He then separated the light from the darkness and called the light day and the darkness níght.

v. Again, God made the expanse and separated the waters under the expanse from the water above and called it sky.

vi. The water below the expanse was then gathered to one place to create dry ground.

vii. The dry ground God called land and the gathered water, seas.

viii. God then caused the land to produce vegetation of plants bearing seeds according to their kind and trees bearing fruits.

ix. Further, God created the seasons, days and years, and lights in the expanse of the sky to give light on the earth.

x. God made two great lights- the greater light to govern the day and the lesser light to govern the night.

xi. This was followed by the creation of the great creatures of the sea and every living and moving thing.

xii. Every winged bird according to its kind was also created to fly across the expanse of the sky.

xiii. The creatures of the sea and the birds were commanded to increase in number to fill the water in the sea and the earth respectively.

xiv. Liveştock, creatures that move along the ground, and wild animals were also created.

xv. God then created man in His own image and likeness to rule over the fish of the sea and the birds of the air and all creatures.

xvi. God blessed the man and woman to be fruitful and increase in number to fill the earth and subdue it.

 

B Ways humans are destroying God's creation:

i. legal mining/galamsey/sand mining.

ii Overgrazing.

iii Pollution -water, air, and noise.

iv. Indiscriminate waste disposal.

v. Over-exploitation of natural resources/ deforestation/overfishing/overhunting.

vi. Genetic engineering (cloning).

vi. Homosexuality/lesbianism.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q:
(a) State the instructions God gave to Joshua on Joshua's assumption of leadership over Israel.
(b) Briefly explain the various lessons political leaders can learn from Joshua's experience.
explanation:
A. God's instructions to Joshua on his assumption of leadership over lsrael (Numbers 27:15-23;Joshua 1:1-9): .

i When Moses was about to exit the leadership position of lsrael, he prayed to God to appoint his Successor in order not to leave the people like sheep without a shepherd.

ii, So, the Lord ordered Moses to take Joshua, the son of Nun, a man in whom is the spirit and lay hand on him.

iii. Joshua was to be brought before Eleazar the priest and the entire assembly to be commissioned.

iv. Moses did as was commanded by the Lord and Joshua was brought before Eleazar the priest and the whole assembly who commissioned and laid hand on him.

V. God then promised to give Joshua every place that the sole of his feet would tread.

vi. The territory extending from the desert to Lebanon and from the great river, Euphrates to the Great Sea.

vi. The Lord promised to be with Joshua as He was with Moses.

vii. And no one would be able to stand against him all the days of his life.

ix. Joshua was to be strong and courageous as he would lead the Israelites to inherit the land promised their forefathers.

x. He was to be careful to obey all the law Moses gave him to be successful wherever he went.

xi. Joshua was to make sure the book of the law never departed from his mouth but to meditate upon it day and night.

xii. He was to apply the law in everything to be prosperous and successful

xiii. Joshua was to be courageous and strong and not to be terrified or discouraged, for the Lord would be with him wherever he went.

 

 

B Lessons political leaders can learn from Joshua's experience:

1. Political leaders should not impose successors but allow God to lead in making such a choice

ii. They should help the people to elect leaders who are goal-oriented and people-oriented

iii Political leaders should know when enough is enough and retire honourably

iv. They should be interested in grooming people who can take over from them.

v Like Moses, they should show confidence in the authority and abilities of their successors to rule

vi. They must be confident that they can only succeed when they observe God's prescriptions

vii Political leaders should be patient as they wait to assume leadership positions and not take power through evil manipulations or by force of arms

viii Political leaders should be courageous and obedient in all they do

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q:
(a) State the different ways David resisted taking Saul's life at the wilderness of Ziph.
(b) Mention four benefits one can derive from self-control.
explanation:
A How David resisted taking Saul's life at the wilderness of Ziph (1 Samuel 26:1-25):

i Saul received a report from the Ziphites that David was hiding on the hill of Hachilah

ii So, Saul went in search of David at the Desert of Ziph with three thousand chosen men

iii Saul and his men made their camp beside the road on the hill of Hachilah facing Jeshimon whilst David stayed in the desert

iv. When David heard of the presence of Saul, he sent men to scout the whereabouts of Saul and this was confirmed.

v. With this information. David and Abishai went to the camp of Saul and saw him and Abner, the commander of the army lying down sleeping with the army encamped around them

vi. Abishai said to David that the Lord had delivered his enemy into head and that David should allow him to kill Saul.

vii. David rebuked Abishai because it is a sin to kill the lords anointed.

viii. David again said that Saul should be allowed to die in the hands of the Lord or in battle but not in his hand / the life of Saul was in God's hands.

ix., David then ordered Sauľ's spear that was stuck to the ground near his head and his water jar to be taken away

x. When David crossed to the other side, he called to Saul and his men saying they had failed to protect the King and that Abner, the Army Commander deserved to die

Xi. Saul then called out to David and he responded by asking why Saul was pursuing him and what his guilt was.

xii. David, however, said if it was the Lord's doing; then He should accept an offering but if it were men. they must be cursed before the Lord.

xiii. David wondered why the king of Israel should be hunting him like a partridge in the mountain.

xiv. Saul apologized to David and promised not to harm him because he had spared his life.

xV. David handed over Saul's spear and water jug to him and noted that the Lord rewards everyone for his/her righteousness and faithfulness.

xvi. David ended by saying that the Lord had delivered Saul into his hand but he would not lay hands on the Lord's anointed.

xvii. Because I valued your life, may the Lord value my life and deliver me from all troubles.

xviii, Saul blessed David and they both departed.

 

B. Benefits of self-control:

i. It is a sign of maturity/principled.

ii. it fosters harmonious living with others.

iii. It prevents reckless talks and actions/avoidance of temptation.

iv. It ensures well-thought out decisions.

v. It accords oneself respect and respect for others.

vi. It ensures peaceful resolution of disputes.

vii. It prevents tension/conflict/war.

viii. It builds trust/confidence.

ix. It promotes good health/healthy life.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q:
(a) Give an account of Solomon's prayer during the dediçation of the temple.
(b) Explain three ways leaders can use their resources for the development of the society.
explanation:
A. Solomon's prayer at the dedication of the temple (1 Kings 8: 1-53):

i  After the completion of the Temple, Solomon assembled all the elders and chiefs of lsrael for the dedication of the Temple.

ii. He had the ark of the Lord brought from Zion and placed beneath the wings of the cherubim situated at the Most Holy place of the Temple.

iii. Standing before the altar of the Lord in front of the Assembly with hands spread towards heaven, Solomon acknowledged the supremacy of God and His faithfulness to His promise to David.

iv. Solomon urged the Lord to keep the promise made to David that there would always be a man to sit before Him on the throne if only his children would be obedient to the Lord,

v. Solomon acknowledged that like the earth and heaven, the Temple could not contain the Lord.

vi. He, however, appealed to the Lord to have mercy and hear his cry and prayer in His presence.

vii. Solomon requested of the Lord to open His eyes towards the Temple day and night so that He would hear the prayer of His servants towards this place.

viii. When a man wrongs his neighbor and is required to take an oath before your altar in this Temple, hear from heaven and act

ix. Judge between servants, condemning the guilty and bringing down on his own head what he has done/declare the innocent not guilty, and so establish his innocence.

x. When Israel is defeated at war due to sin against you but they confess to your name in the Temple, bring them again to the land of their forefathers,

xi. If there is drought or famine due to sins of your people and they turn to you in supplication in the Temple, give them rain.

xii. In times of disaster or disease, when the people pray with hands towards the Temple, forgive and deliver them.

xiii. When a foreigner hears of your name and comes to pray towards the Temple, grant his request so that all the people of the earth would know you.

xiv. Let Israel win all its wars when they pray towards your city and the Temple that i have built for your name.

xv Even when Israel is taken into captivity due to your anger and they repent and pray towards your chosen city and the Temple, hear their prayers and uphold their cause.

 

B. Ways leaders can use resources for the development of society:

i. Provision of social amenities.

ii. Investing in the marginalized society.

iii, Avoiding corruption/mismanagement.

iv. Equitable distribution of resources.

v. Education and training for all persons.

vi. Avoiding unnecessary litigation and wars/conflict.

Vii. Investing in projects that take into consideration generations yet unborn.

viii. Protection of women's and children's rights.

ix. Ensuring religious freedom.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q:
(a) Discuss the circumstances that led to Jesus saying "Do not be afraid; henceforth you will be catching men
(b) Enumerate three factors that prevent people from becoming committed Christians.
explanation:
A "Do not be afraid: henceforth you will be catching men" The Circumstance that led to the statement (Luke 5:1-11:

İ. Jesus stood by the lake Gennesaret and the people gathered to hear the word of God from him.

ii. Jesus saw two empty boats and entered one of them belonging to Simon Peter.

iii Jesus stood in the boat and taught the people around.

iv. After the teaching, Jesus instructed Peter to cast his nets in the deep waters for a catch.

v. Simon initially protested but later agreed to do as Jesus directed.

vi. After he threw the nets, he caught a great shoal of fish.

vi. His nets were breaking, and he had to request assistance from his colleagues.

vii. They filled the two boats so that they began to sink.

viii. Peter was so much astonished that he begged Jesus to depart from him since he was a sinner.

ix. Peter was in the company of James and John, the Sons of Zebedee who were also fishermen.

x. Jesus then invited them to be fishers of men.

xi. And instantly, they left everything and followed Jesus.

 

B. Factors that prevent people from becoming committed Christians:

i. Lack of self-denial.

ii. Poverty

iii. Lack of self-control.

iv. Unforgiveness/intolerance.

v. Unwilling to give up sinful habits/pride.

vi. Wealth materialism.

vii. Not praying regularly.

viii. Not reading the Bible regularly.

ix. Failure to abide by the word of God/lack of faith.

x. Peer pressure/Bad company.

xi.idolatry.

xii. Demonic/satanic influence.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q:
(a) Give an account of the burial of Jesus as narrated by the Synoptic Gospels.
(b) Mention three ways money is overspent during funerals in society.
explanation:
The burial of Jesus as narrated by the Synoptic Gospels (Matthew 27:57-66: Mark 15:42-47: Luke 23:50-56):

i. Jesus was condemned to death by the Sanhedrin and Pilate.

ii. Jesus was crucified between two robbers at Golgotha.

iii. He was crucified on the preparation day, that is, the day before the Sabbath.

iv. Joseph of Arimathea, a rich/good and righteous man, was opposed to the Sanhedrin's condemnation of Jesus.

v. On the evening of the crucifixion, Joseph of Arimathea, a member of the Sanhedrin, went to Pilate and asked for the body of Jesus.

vi. Pilate granted the request of Joseph and gave the boby to him.

vii. Joseph wrapped the body in a linen shroud.

viii. The body was then laid in a new rock hewed tomb belonging to Joseph.

ix A stone was was then rolled against the door of the tomb.

x. The chief priest and Pharisees requested Pilate to deploy guards to secure the tomb of Jesus.

xi. According to them, Jesus had claimed to rise from the grave after three days.

xii. The women returned to prepare spices and ointments to anoint Jesus' body.

xiii. Mary Magdalene, Mary the mother of James, and some other women from Galilee were at the tomb to see where Jesus was laid.

xiv. According to Matthew's account, Pilate ordered that guards should be sent to secure the tomb.

xv. AIl this was based on the fact that it was alleged that the disciples intended to go and steal Jesus' body.

 

B. Way money is overspent during funerals in the society:

i. Buying special funeral clothes.

ii. Buying expensive coffins.

iii. Preparing special food for mourners - take away/buffet.

iv. Keeping corpse in the mortuary for a long time.

v. Funeral announcements on the radio and T.V., newspaper/billboards/banners.

vi. Employing special undertakers/mourners, pallbearers.

vii. Preparing special graves.

viii. Expensive entertainment/hiring of expensive venues 

ix provision of sovenirs

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q:
(a) Narrate the circumstances under which Peter was rescued from prison.
(b) Mention any three lessons that can be learnt from the above incident.
explanation:
A. Circumstances under which Peter was rescued from prison. (Acts 12:1-19):

i.Herod Agrippa joined in the opposition to the gospel by persecuting the disciples to please the Jews. .

ii He had James the brother of John killed.

iii. After James' death, he realized that the Jews were happy.

iv. And in order to please them the more. he had Peter imprisoned.

v. Herod's intention was to have Peter killed after the feast of unleavened bread.

vi. The night prior to the day Herod planned to kill Peter, Peter was sleeping between two soldiers.

vii. Suddenly, an angel of the Lord appeared to Peter and asked him to dress up and put on his shoes.

viii. While in prison, fervent prayers were said for Peter by the brethren.

ix. The angel commanded Peter to wrap his mantle and follow him.

x. Peter followed the angel through the first and second gates.

xi. When Peter got to the Iron Gate leading to the City, the gate opened on its own accord.

xii. And just as they went out, the angel left Peter and he realized that it was an angel of the Lord that came to rescue him.

xiii. Peter went to Mary's house and when knocked at the door, Rhoda saw and she announced his presence to the brethren.

xiv However, the brethren did not believe her and thought she was mad.

XV. The brethren insisted that it was peters angel that she saw.

xvi. Peter then joined the brethren who were praying for him in the house of Mary the mother of John Mark.

 

B Lessons that can be learned from the incident in 7A above:

i. The united fervent prayer of the church has a great effect.

ii. The church should learn to petition God and intercede for those in crisis.

iii. God still answers the prayers of the faithful ones.

iv. God can still save his people in any difficult situation.

v. We should have no doubt when we pray/believe in our prayers.

vi. In every situation, God is still with us.

vii. The need to trust in God no matter the circumstance.

viii. The supremacy of God prevails over all other authorities.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q:
(a) Explain, in detail, how James addressed the subject of prayer.
(b) Mention at least two roles prayer can play in the life of a Christian.
explanation:
A. How James addressed the subịect of prayer (James 1:2-8; 5: 13-18):

i. James admonished believers to avoid sin since God detests it so sinners must confess and repent.

ii. He encouraged Christians to be joyful in times of trials.

iii. According to James, trials, trials are meant to test one's faith.

iv. And when one's faith IS fully tested, it produces steadfastness.

V. And steadfastness lead to perfection and one lacking nothing.

VI. James encouraged those who lack the wisdom to pray to God who gives without reproach.

vii. He said prayers should be said in faith without doubt for those who doubt will receive nothing.

viii. James compares the one who doubts to the wave of a sea that is driven by the wind.

ix. He further said that prayers must be made according to the will of God,

x. He also urged Christians to exercise patience when praying for God's will to be done at the right time.

xi. James advised Čhristians to pray in humility.

xii. He again said that there should be persistence When one prays.

xiii. Christians should do away with double-mindedness when praying.

xiv. James is of the VIew that anyone suffering should pray, and the cheerful should sing hymns of praise.

xv. He again remarked the sick should be anointed with oil and prayed for by the elders

xvi From James point of view, prayer made in faith will bring healing to the sick

xvii James also staes that a righteous man's prayer has great power in its effects.

xviii. in conclusion. James states that Elijah prayed that it should not rain for three and half years and it did not rain.

xix. And he again prayed that it should rain and it did

 

B Roles prayer can play in the life of a Christian:

i. It helps one to make requests to God.

ii. One has the privilege to thank God.

iii. One is quipped to fight the devil.

İv. Prayer is a source of spiritual strength to lead a good Christian life.

v. One's sins can be forgiven through prayer.

vi. It gives the believer hope.

vii. Through prayer, We commune and fellowship with God.

viii. It serves as a medium to adore God.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q:
(a) State in detail, all that Peter expected from the people when he said, "But you are a chosen race, a Royal Priesthood, a holy nation".

(b) Enumerate four factors that prevent Christians from doing the will of God.
explanation:
A "But you are a chosen race, a Royal Priesthood, a holy nation"The expectations of Peter from the people in reference to this quote:

 

i. Peter says Christians have been transformed from darkness into light so they should declare the wonderful deeds of God.

ii. He said formerly, Christians were no people but they are now God's people who have received mercý.

iii. Peter appealed to Christians not to conform to the standards of this world.

iv. He admonished them to avoid the passions of the flesh and things that hinder their spiritual growth.

v. He was of the view that believers must maintain good conduct among non-Christians so that through their deeds, God will be glorified.

vi. Peter holds the view that, Christians are to be subject to every human institution/authority.

vii. According to Peter, Christians should not misuse the freedom they have to do evil.

viii. The believer, as Peter states, should honour all men.

ix. He again states that Christians should love their brothers and fear God.

x. The Christian should honour the one who leads him.

xi. Peter also admonished servants to submit themselves to their masters.

xii. AS a chosen race, Christians are advised by Peter to have patience when they suffer for no wrong done.

xiii. He continued that Christians should emulate the example of Christ who suffered for no wrong done.

xiv. Finally, Peter encouraged Christians to die to sin and live to righteousness since Christ died on the cross for our sins.

 

B. Factors that prevent Christians from doing the will of God:

 

i.Failure to read the Bible always/ignorance of the word of God.

ii. Disobedience to the word of God.

iii. Living in sin/immorality.

iv. Prayerlessness.

V. Negative peer pressure.

vi. Unforgiveness/hatred.

vii. Persecution.

viii. Hostile environment.

ix. Religious intolerance.

x. Worldliness/materialism/greed.

xi. Negative cultural/traditional practices.

explanation: Hiram was the king of Tyre during the time of King Solomon.  In 1 Kings 5:1-12, it is mentioned that Hiram and Solomon entered into a friendly alliance. Hiram sent his servants to Solomon, and they, along with Solomon's workers, cut down cedar and cypress trees from the forests of Lebanon for the construction of the Temple in Jerusalem. Hiram's assistance was crucial for Solomon in obtaining high-quality timber for the temple's construction.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q: Two kings of Persia who passed decrees to bring about the rebuilding of the temple in Jerusalem were
explanation: The correct answer is B. Darius and Cyrus. These two Persian kings issued decrees that allowed for the rebuilding of the temple in Jerusalem. The decree of Cyrus is recorded in the book of Ezra 1:1-4, where Cyrus, the king of Persia, issues a proclamation allowing the Jews to return to Jerusalem and rebuild the temple. The decree of Darius is mentioned in the book of Ezra 6:1-12. In this passage, Darius confirms and strengthens the decree of Cyrus, ensuring that the temple reconstruction continues despite opposition.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q: The Jew who came from exile to Jerusalem to study the law of the Lord and teach others was
explanation: Ezra is known for his dedication to studying and teaching the Law of Moses. He led a group of Jewish exiles back to Jerusalem from Babylon during the Persian Empire period, and he played a crucial role in promoting the understanding and observance of the law among the returned exiles.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q: According to Luke's gospel, when the Devil had ended the temptation of Jesus, the Devil departed
options: and Jesus was baptized in the Jordan
to work out a better strategy
and Angels came to minister to Jesus because he had accomplished His mission
explanation:
In the Gospel of Luke, after the Devil had tempted Jesus in the wilderness, we find the account in Luke 4:13 (NIV):

"When the devil had finished all this tempting, he left him until an opportune time."

The statement implies that the Devil departed, it is commonly interpreted that the Devil left with a sense of defeat and embarrassment because Jesus successfully resisted all the temptations. The departure of the Devils means that Jesus overcame the temptations and remained faithful to God's will. The idea is that the Devil retreated temporarily, waiting for another opportunity to tempt Jesus.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q:
2a. List some of Joseph's first encounters with Pharaoh

2b. State some lessons leaders can learn from Joseph
explanation:
2a. (Gen. 41:1-45)


i. Joseph was imprisoned by Potiphar after he was falsely accused of rape by Potiphar's wife

ii. Pharaoh had a dream that he stood by the River Nile and saw seven fat cows and seven lean cows all coming from the river

iii. The fat cows were swallowed by the thin, lean cows

iv. He also saw seven thin ears of corn come up on another stalk

v. And the seven thin ears of corn ate up the seven good ones

vi. When Pharaoh woke up, he summoned his magicians and wise men to interpret the dreams but they could not.

vii. At this juncture, Pharaoh's chief butlet remembered how Joseph had interpreted his dreams in prison two years ago

- And upon the recomendation of the butler, Pharaoh sent for Joseph from prison to interpret his dreams.

- Pharaoh narrated the dreams to him and Joseph said it was only God who gives favourable interpretation of dreams.

- Joseph then told Pharaoh that the two dreams were the same and that the seven fat cows and seven good ears of corn stood for seven years of abundant harvest in Egypt

- Joseph further said that the seven lean cows and seven thin ears of corn represent seven years of famine in Egypt etc.

 

2b.

- He was a visionary

- He was humble

- He was patient

- He had administrative equalities

- He had a positive attitude towards misfortune

- using his talent for the benefit of other etc.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q:
5a. What are the events that took place after Herod Agrippai killed James?

5b. What are the factors that motivate Christians to pray?
explanation:
5a. (Acts i2:1-19)

- In his determination to persecute the Church, Herod laid violent hands on some members.

- he killed James, the brother of John

- This act pleased the Jews

- Herod, who wanted to please the Jews more, arrested Peter and intended to kill him after the Passover

- Whilst in prison, Peter was chained and being guarded by four groups of soldiers so that he would not escape.

- The brethren met in the house of Mary the mother of the disciple called John mark to pray for Peter's safety etc.

 

5b. 

Several factors motivate Christians to pray, including:

  1. Communication with God: Prayer is a means for Christians to communicate with God, express their thoughts, feelings and desires, and seek guidance.

  2. Dependence on God: Recognising their need for divine help in daily life, challenges and decisions, Christians are motivated to pray for God’s intervention.

  3. Worship and Praise: Christians pray to worship and praise God for His greatness and faithfulness.

  4. Seeking Forgiveness: Prayer is a channel for repentance, where Christians ask for forgiveness for their sins and receive God's grace.

  5. Intercession for Others: Christians are often motivated by a desire to intercede for others, praying for their well-being, salvation or healing.

  6. Thanksgiving: Gratitude for blessings and answered prayers drives Christians to continue in prayer.

  7. Obedience to God’s Word: Many Christians are motivated to pray because it is commanded in the Bible (e.g., 1 Thessalonians 5:17, "pray without ceasing").

  8. Strength in Times of Trouble: During trials and tribulations, prayer offers comfort, peace, and the strength to endure difficult circumstances.

- Employees who are discriminated against may feel unappreciated by their employers

- It leads to social vices

- It stifles initiative etc.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q: Which of the following names was given to Solomon by the prophet Nathan when he was born?
explanation:
The name Jedidiah was given to Solomon by the prophet Nathan, and it carries a significant meaning. The event is recorded in 2 Samuel 12:24-25. Here's a brief explanation:

After King David committed adultery with Bathsheba and arranged for her husband Uriah to be killed, the prophet Nathan confronted David with his sin. As a consequence of David's actions, the child born to him and Bathsheba became ill and eventually died.

After the child's death, Solomon was born later, and the name Jedidiah ("beloved of the Lord") was given to him by the prophet Nathan (2 Samuel 12:24-25). The naming of Solomon as Jedidiah served as a reminder of God's love and grace even in the midst of the consequences of David's wrongdoing. It emphasizes that God had a special and affectionate purpose for Solomon, who would later become known for his wisdom and play a crucial role in the construction of the temple in Jerusalem.

Solomon, after offering sacrifices at the high place in Gibeon, had a dream in which God appeared to him and granted him the opportunity to request anything he desired. Solomon asked for wisdom, and God not only granted him wisdom but also wealth and honour.

explanation:
"My soul is very sorrowful, even to death; remain here and watch with me," is found in the Gospel of Matthew 26:38 (RSV):

"Then he said to them, 'My soul is very sorrowful, even to death; remain here, and watch with me.'"

This event took place in the Garden of Gethsemane, just before Jesus' arrest and subsequent crucifixion.

people are punished not for their sins

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q: O foolish men, and slow of heart to believe all that the prophets have spoken! Which people were being referred to as foolish? The
options: women who went to the tomb of Jesus.
explanation: The statement "O foolish men, and slow of heart to believe all that the prophets have spoken!" was addressed by Jesus to the two men traveling to Emmaus. This is recorded in Luke 24:25, where Jesus, unrecognized by these men on the road to Emmaus, rebukes them for their lack of understanding and belief in the fulfilment of the prophetic Scriptures regarding the Messiah.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q: Which of the following qualified Matthias as an apostle to replace Judas Iscariot? He
options: had accompanied the disciples from the time of the baptism of Jesus
took active part in evangelism from house to house
was among the early disciples who prayed fervently
was among the one hundred and twenty disciples who had the Holy Spirit
explanation: Matthias was chosen to replace Judas Iscariot as an apostle because he met the qualifications set by Peter and the early disciples. According to Acts 1:21-22 (RSV), the replacement for Judas had to be someone who had been with Jesus from the time of His baptism by John until His ascension and could serve as a witness to His resurrection.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q: The replacement of Judas Iscariot was necessary because the
options: death of Judas Iscariot was premature
twelve disciples represented the twelve tribes of Israel
prophesies of the scripture needed to be fulfilled
status quo of the twelve must be maintained
explanation:
According to Acts 1:15-26, the replacement of Judas Iscariot was necessary to fulfil the Scriptures. The disciples believed that it was in line with the prophetic writings of the Old Testament, particularly Psalms 69:25 and 109:8, which were quoted in Acts 1:20 (RSV):

20 For it is written in the book of Psalms,

‘Let his habitation become desolate,
and let there be no one to live in it’;

and

‘His office let another take.’

These passages were interpreted as foretelling the need for another to replace Judas among the apostles.

explanation: The statement that God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble is found in 1 Peter 5:5. subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q:
Themes From The Old Testament

 

(a) Give an account of the story of the crossing of the Red Sea by the Israelites under the leadership of Moses.

(b) Mention three anc the lessons that can be learnt about the nature of the God from the above incident.
explanation:
(Exodus 13:17-22, 14:1-31)

(i) Pharaoh allowed the Israelites to leave Egypt into the wilderness to worship God after years resistance,

(ii) He thought they would be on a three-day journey as they had earlier requested

(iii) Pharaoh never knew that they intended to leave forever,

(iv) When Pharaoh was informed that the Israelites had fled, he regretted allowing them to go.

(v) Pharaoh the and his men took their chariots and chased ee the Israelites.

(vi) God hardened Pharaoh's of heart and he pursued the Israelites till he caught up with them at the Red Sea.

(vii) The Israelites feared greatly when the Egyptians were just behind them and Red Sea was before them.

(viii) They complained to Moses that he had brought them to the wilderness to die.

(ix) The Israelites further said they would have preferred serving the Egyptians in Egypt to dying in the wilderness.

(x) Moses encouraged them not to be afraid but to stand firm and see the salvation of the Lord.

(xi) they were seeing that day, would never be seen again.

(xi) According to Moses, the Lord would fight for them and that they should be still.

(xiii) God told Moses to inform the the Israelites not to cry but move forward.

(xiv) Moses was asked by the Lord. to lift up his rod and stretch his "hand over the sea (to divide it).

(x) The Lord hardened the hearts of the Egyptians so they would go into the sea to catch up with the Israelites.

(xvi) At this point, the angel of God who was in front of the Israelites went behind them in the form of a pillar of cloud.

(xvii) Moses then stretched out his hand over the sea as commanded and it parted creating a dry land,

(xviii) The Israelites then walked on the dry land across the sea to the other side.

(xix) The Egyptians chased them through the dry land but Moses stretched his hand, the sea merged killing Pharaoh and his people.

 

(b) Nature of God:

(i) God is Omnipresent.

(i)God is Omnipotent

(iii)Nature responds to God

(iv) God is always faithful to his people

(v) God provides for his people.

(vi) It shows how gracious God is

(vi)God is Omniscience.

(viii) God protects his people. 

(ix) It shows the sovereignty of God.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q:
Themes From The Old Testament

(a) Relate the events that led to the Providing pronouncement of punishment on the household of Eli.

(b) State three ways by in their which parents can make their children responsible.
explanation:
(a)(1 Sam 2:11-36):

(i) Eli, the priest of God had 'two sons; Hophni and Phinehas.

(ii) These sons were also priests of God.

(iii) However, Eli's sons were worthless men who had no regards for the Lord.

(iv) The priest could only take sacrificial meat while it was being boiled.

(v) With a three-pronged fork, the servants of the priest would take for himself whatever the fork brought up.

(vi) However, the sons of Eli would reject this law and would rather demand raw meat.

(vii) Even when their attention was drawn to the law, they would forcefully take whatever portion of meat they wanted.

(viii) This sin of Eli's sons was very great in the sight of the lord for they treated the Lord's sacrifice with contempt.

(ix) Meanwhile, Samuel, a small boy living with Eli kept the law and was faithfully ministering before the Lord.

(x) Eli was well aware of the depraved life of his sons who even slept with the women serving at the entrance of the tent of meeting.

(xi) Eli drew the attention of his sons to the enormity of their sins and encouraged them to repent,

(xii) However, the sons' of Eli did not listen to their father' rebuke, for it was the Lord's will to put them to death.

(xiii) Now, a man of God came to Eli to remind him of the Lord's relationship with Israel and how He chose Eli's father as a priest to serve in His presence.

(xiv) He further expressed God's disappointment at how Eli honoured his sons over God's sacrifices.

(xv) He declared that even though, the Lord promised the house of Eli's father to be priests forever, He would honour only those who honour Him and despise those who despise Him.

(xvi) The Lord would cut short the strength of Eli's household and there would not be any old man in the family line (xvii) Furthermore, those who would be spared would only blind their eyes with tears and grieve their hearts, and all their descendants would die in the prime of their lives.

(xviii) Your two sons would die on the the same day and in their place, i would raise a faithful priest who would do what is on my heart

(xix) The new priest would be firmly established on Eli's household would bow down before him for sustenance.

 

(b) (i) Assigning responsibilities to them.

(ii) Providing counseling and guidance.

(iii) Adequate supervision.

(iv) Showing interest in their affairs.

(v) Involving children decision-making.

(vi) Adequate provision of their needs.

(vi) Adequate formal education.

(viii) Being good role models to them.

(ix) Showing love and affection to them.

(x) Willingness to correct them when they go wrong.

(xi) Provision of religious and moral training.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q:
Themes From The Old Testament

 

(a) I have done wrong; return, my son David, for I will no more do you harm ... Recount the events that led to this apology.

(b) What two qualities can we derive from David's attitude?
explanation:
(a) (1 Sam 26 :1 - 25):

(i) To escape from Saul. David went to hide in the wilderness of Ziph.

(ii) The Ziphites informed Saul of the presence of David in the wilderness.

(iii) Saul went to the wilderness of Ziph with three thousand soldiers in search of David,

(iv) When David got to know about Saul's plans, he sent spies to confirm the presence of Saul in the wilderness.

(v) David and Abishai went in the night to where Saul was sleeping with Abner, his commander and his men of war.

(vi) Abishai sought permission from David to kill Saul.

(vii) David restrained him saying he will not spill the blood of the Lord's anointed.

(viii) David ordered Abishai to remove Saul's spear and jar of water.

(ix) Nobody saw them as all the people in the camp of Saul, were deeply asleep including Abner, his chief bodyguard.

(x) When David had gone far, he called out Abner and reprimanded him For his failure to keep proper watch over his master, Saul.

(xi) He said, Abner deserved to die for the security lapses.

(xii) David asked him about Saul's spear and jar of water.

(xiii) While David was reprimanding Abner, Saul recognized David's voice and he called out to him.

(xiv) David demanded to know why Saul wanted to kill him for no just cause.

(xv) Saul felt guilty and repented of his sins and promised, never to harm David.

(xvi) Saul appreciated the way David spared his life and David then returned the spear and jar of water he had collected

(xvii) Saul blessed David abundantly

 

(b) Qualities of David;

(i) We must forgive those who offend us David had every opportunity to he Kill Saul, yet he spared him.

(ii) David demonstrated his submission to the will of God. He said he would not spill the blood of the Lord's anointed.

(iii) The need to fear God. It's reflected in David's decision not to slay the Lord's anointed.

(iv) We must show love to everyone around us.

(v) David showed good leadership by making sure that his making subordinate obeyed him. Eg. Abishai.

(vi) David trusted God to save/protect him from his enemies.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q:
Themes From The Old Testament

 

(a) Recount Josiah's response to the discovery of the Book of the Law in the temple.

(b) Identify three ways in which the religious situation today is similar to Josiah's days.
explanation:
(a)(2 Kings 22, 23:1-30):

(i) Josiah was eight years old when he begin to reign.

(ii) He did what was right in the eyes of the Lord, and walked in all the ways of David his father.

(iii) During the eighteenth year of Josiah's reign, he asked that the temple should be renovated,

(iv) When the temple was being repaired, a copy of the book of the Law was discovered by Hilkiah, the High Priest.

(v) Hilkiah then gave the book to Shaphan, the king's secretary and he read it to the hearing of the king.

(vi) When the king heard the words of the book of the Law, he rent his clothes.

(vii) The king sent Hilkiah to go and find out from the Lord concerning the words of the book that was found,

(viii) Huldah, the prophetess was then consulted and she said that God would bring evil upon Judah. Because Judah had forsaken the Lord and had burned incense to other gods,

(x) She said King Josiah would be spared because his heart was penitent and the evil that will befall Judah would not happen in his days,

(xi) Josiah then summoned the elders to the temple and read the Law to them,

(xii) King Josiah then began is religious reforms by purging Judah of foreign and religious practices,

(xiii) All vessels and items made for Baal and Asheran were removed from the temple and burnt,

(xiv) All the priests who had been burning incense in the high places to Baal were killed,

(xv) All the houses built for the cult prostitutes were pulled down.

(xvi) The celebration of the Passover was reinstated

 

(b)i) There are cultic practices and secret societies in the society and churches today as it was in the days of Josiah. (in Many worshippers in our churches today involve themselves in idol worship as in the days of Josjan.

(iii) Some pastors and church leaders indulge in immoral acts and unchristian practices as the priests of Josiah's time did.

(iv) There are corrupt practices, in the society and church as it happened in the days of Josiah.

(v) Commercialization of the gospel (e.g. ritual bathing, use of effigies, fake miracles, etc.).

(vi) Activities of fake pastors,

(vii) Syncretism.

(viii) Religious facade.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q:
THEMES FROM THE SYNOPTIC GOSPELS AND THE ACTS OF THE APOSTLES

 

(a) My soul is very sorrowful, even to death; remain here, and watch with me. Give an account of what happened to Jesus after he made this statement.

(b) State two ways by which one can assist people going through challenges.
explanation:
(a) (Matt 26: 36-56, Mark14:32:52):

(i) Jesus and his disciples went to the garden of Gethsemane to pray

(ii) He took with him Peter, James and John to another place leaving the rest behind.

(iii) Jesus then left the three and went further to pray alone.

(iv) He prayed saying my father, if it be possible; let this cup pass from me, nevertheless, not as I will, but as thou wilt.

(v) Jesus came back to the three and found them sleeping and He asked Peter whether they could not watch with Him for one hour.

(vi) Jesus then told them to that they would not enter into temptation/the spirit was willing but the flesh is weak

(vii) Again, for the second time Jesus went away to play au if it was possible the-cup should pass by him.

(viii) Jesus came back and found the three disciples asleep again.

(ix)He left them and went to pray for the third time and He came back to meet them asleep.

(×) He asked them whether they were still sleeping and taking their rest.

(xi) Jesus then told them that the hour was at hand and the son of man would be betrayed into the hands of sinners.

(xii)He made them aware that the betrayer was at hand so they should get ready to go.

(xiii) As Jesus was speaking, Judas Iscariot led a crowd with swords and clubs.

(xiv) The crowd attempted arresting Jesus after Judas had kissed him.

(xv) One of Jesus' disciples drew out a sword and cut off the ears of a servant of the high priest.

(xvi) Jesus rebuked the disciple saying all who take the sword perish by the sword.

(xvii) He then fixed the ear of the servant back

(xviii)Jesus was then arrested and taken to Caiaphas.

(xix) All the disciples except Peter deserted him

 

(b) (i) One has to visit the one going through the challenge.

(ii) One should pray with them.

(iii) One should assist the person in cash or kind when necessary.

(iv) Counseling the victim.

(v) Relocating/changing the victim's environment.

(vi) Seeking redress/justice for the victim.

(vii) Advocacy

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q:
THEMES FROM THE SYNOPTIC GOSPELS AND THE ACTS OF THE APOSTLES

(a) You wicked servant; I forgave you all that debt because you besought me … Outline the circumstances that led to the above statement.

(b) State three reasons for which people find it difficult to forgive.
explanation:
(a) Jesus' Teaching on Forgiveness (Matt. 18: 21-35):

(i) Peter wanted to know from Jesus whether he should forgive his neighbour seven times.

(ii) Jesus responded by saying seventy times seven.

(iii) Jesus then told a parable comparing the kingdom of heaven to a king who wished to settle accounts with his servants.

(iv) And in the process, a servant who owed the King ten thousand talents was brought before him.

(v) Because he could not pay the money, the king ordered him to be sold with his family until the last money was paid.

(vi) The servant fell on his knees and begged the king to have patience with him and he would pay later,

(vi) Out of pity, the king released the servant and forgave him all the debt

(viii) As the servant went out, he met a fellow servant who owed him hundred denarii.

(ix) He instantly seized him by the throat demanding that he should pay what he owed him.

(x) His fellow servant begged him to have patience and that he would pay later.

(xi) But he refused and put him in prison till he paid the debt,

(xii) When his fellow servants saw what had taken place, they were disturbed and reported to the king.

(xiii) The king invited the first servant, whose debt he cancelled and reprimanded him.

(xiv) He said, Tie was a wicked servant and that he ought to have forgiven his fellow servant and had mercy on him as he did to him.

(xv) Out of anger, the king asked that he should be jailed until he paid the last money.

(xvi) Jesus ended by saying that anyone who did not forgive his fellow man from his heart would also not be forgiven by his heavenly Father.

 

(b) (i) Arrogance/pride.

(ii) Consistency of the offense.

(iii) The enormity/gravity of the offense.

(iv) Peer influence.

(v) Immaturity

(vi) Self-centeredness.

(vii) The emotional state of the offended/wickedness/hard heartedness.

(viii) Ignorance of the word of God.

(ix) Envy/hatred.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q:
THEMES FROM THE SYNOPTIC GOSPELS AND THE ACTS OF THE APOSTLES

 

(a) Give an account of how the prophecy of Joel was fulfilled on the day of Pentecost.

(b) In what two ways was the above incident significant?
explanation:
(a) (Acts 2:1 -41):

(i) On the day of Pentecost, the apostles were together in the upper room,

(ii) Suddenly, a sound from heaven like that of a rushing mighty wind filled the place where they were gathered

(iii) There appeared on their heads, tongues as of fire which rested upon each of them.

(iv)They were all filled with the Holy Spirit and they started speaking in other tongues as the Spirit gave them utterance.

(v) There were many pilgrims from different nations of the world in Jerusalem that day.

(vi)They converged together to listen to the apostles but they were amazed as each heard the apostles speak in his own language,

(vi) While many of the pilgrims wondered on the incident, some mocked them saying, they were already drunk.

(viii) Peter stood up to refute the allegation of drunkenness.

(ix) He said they were not drunk as the people had thought,

(x) But they were speaking in tongues in line with the outpouring of the Holy Spirit as prophesied by Joel.

(xi) That on the last days." He would pour out His Spirit upon all flesh people would prophesy and there would be signs and wonders.

(xii) Peter further recounted the signs and wonders God had accomplished through Jesus Christ and his crucifixion and resurrection.

(xiii) To support the resurrection of Jesus, Peter quoted the Psalm of David which says "for thou wilt not abandon my soul to Hades nor let thy Holy one see corruption".

(xiv) Peter said the people were Witnesses to the resurrection of Jesus Christ,

(xv) Peter said David was speaking about the resurrection of Christ that he would not be abandoned to Hades that his flesh would not see corruption.

(xvi) As 'Peter concluded his speech, the Jerusalem pilgrims listening to him were touched and they inquired from him what they should do.

(xvii) Peter advised them to repent from their sins and be baptized for the forgiveness of their sins.

(xviii) Three thousand people repented; they were all baptized. (xix) They joined the apostles and received the Holy Spirit

 

(b)(i) The receipt of the Holy Spirit at Pentecost gave the apostles the courage and boldness to preach the gospel.

(ii) Most of the Jerusalem pilgrims repented and were baptized

(iii) Speaking in tongues symbolizes the universalism of the gospel.

(iv) The incident at Pentecost marked the beginning of the church.

(v) The lives of the apostles changed; the Holy Spirit turned them into dynamic apostles.

(vi)The apostles were filled with the resurrection power of Jesus Christ.

(vi) The apostles stood up against anyone that opposed the preaching of the gospel.

(viii) The outpouring of the Holy Spirit was a fulfillment of Jesus' promise to the apostles.

(ix) The apostles were empowered to perform signs and wonders and to prophesy.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q:
THEMES FROM SELECTED EPISTLES

(a) Recount the teachings of James in which he remarked that love your neighbour as yourself.

(b) Identify three negative effects of discrimination among Christians.
explanation:
(a)(James 2:1-13):

(i) James admonished Christians not to show' partiality to anyone but to treat people equally as God expects.

(ii). James holds the view that all Christians are ambassadors of Christ who have the faith of Christ.

(iii) James gave an illustration of a rich man who comes into a Christian gathering, with golden ring and fine clothes and is given special attention

(iv) At the same time, a poor man shabbily dressed also comes in and is treated with contempt.

(v) James is of the view that both the rich man and the poor man should be treated equally without discrimination.

(vi) He says if preferential treating is given to the rich man over the poor man then one is guilty of partiality. According to James,

(vii) showing partiality amounts to causing disunity and assuming the role of judges with evil intention.

(viii) Hence, to James, no distinction should be made among the people because God Himself does not discriminate. (ix) James said God has chosen the poor in the land to be rich in faith.

(×) He stressed that the poor are the heirs of the kingdom which is promised to those who love God.

(xi) He further stated that it is the rich who oppress the poor and drag them to court

(xii) Furthermore, James says that the rich blaspheme the honourable name of God.

(xiii) He therefore concluded that discrimination against the poor and favouring the rich was wrong and unacceptable.

(xiv) James hold the view that anyone who keeps the whole law but fails in one has become guilty of all.

(xv) This is due to the fact that the same God, who said we should not commit adultery, said we should not kill.

(xvi) He finally urged Christians to speak and act as those who are to be judged under the law of liberty.

(xvii) For judgment is without mercy to those who show no mercy

(xviii) However, mercy triumphs over judgment.

 

(b) (i) It creates division in the church/Disunity,

(ii) It could lead to break away of members/ Loss of faith in God.

(ill) It inhibits spiritual growth among Christians.

(iv) It discourages unbelievers from joining the church.

(v) It brings about unhealthy rivalry and discord in the church.

(vi)It leads to segregation in the church.

(vi) It stifles development of talents.

(viii) It leads to favouritism/nepotism,

(ix) It breeds undemocratic tendencies in the church.

(x) It breeds inferiority complex /Lack of confidence/Absence of belongingness.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q:
THEMES FROM SELECTED EPISTLES

 

(a) Outline Peter's recommendations that believers should interact with unbelievers in the society.

(b) State three ways by which Christians can live above reproach in the society.
explanation:
(a) 1 Peter 2:1-25):

(i) Peter admonished Christians living among non-Christians to put away all malice, guile, insincerity, envy and all slander.

(ii) He said, they should behave like the new born babies and long for the to pure spiritual milk so as to grow up to salvation.

(iii) Peter referred to believers as a chosen race, a royal priesthood and a holy nation.

(iv) Peter again said, Christians were God's own people and as such they should declare the wonderful deeds of Him who has called them from darkness into light.

(v) He remarked that some time past Christians were no people but now they are God's people and have received mercy

(vi) Peter appealed to Christians not to conform to the standards of this world.

(vi) They should also avoid the passion of the flesh and things that hinder their spiritual growth

(vii) Christians must also maintain good conduct among non-Christians so that in case of false accusation people would see their good deeds and glorify God.

(ix) Again, Peter advises Christians to be subject to every human institution and authority set up by God,

(x) And by so doing, they would put to silence the ignorance of foolish men.

(xi) Peter advised Christians that as men who live under the freedom of Christ, they must not use their freedom as pretext for evil.

(xii) Again, as servants of God, Christians should honour all men and love the brotherhood.

(xiii) They should also fear God and honour their leaders/emperors/elders.

(xiv) Peter also urged servants to be submissive to their masters both the kind and the overbearing.

(xv) He made Christians aware that they could suffer for doing the right thing but they must not be worried,

(xvi) Finally, he said Christians should follow the good example of Jesus who trusted God even in his suffering.

 

(b) (i) Practicing good work ethics.

(ii) Avoiding social vices/Disgraceful conducts,

(iii) Being law/-abiding.

(iv) Performing their civic responsibilities,

(v) Reporting criminal activities to law enforcement agencies.

(vi) Being tolerant of others,

(vii) Being the conscience/Mouthpiece of society.

(viii) Practicing the teachings of the Christian faith/Being Godfearing.

(ix) Avoiding corrupt practices.

(x) Engaging in charity works.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q: On the fifth day, the manifestation of the power of God's word was evident in the creation of the
explanation: According to Genesis, on the fifth day of creation, God said, "And God said, 'Let the waters bring forth swarms of living creatures, and let birds fly above the earth across the firmament of the heavens.'"(Genesis 1:20 - RSV).

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q: Which of the following was God's instruction to Adam after creation? To eat
options: of any tree except that of the knowledge of good and evil.
freely of every tree except the ones with apples.
from the tree in the middle of the garden.
any animal and plant in Eden.
explanation:
In Genesis, God's instruction to Adam regarding eating is presented as follows:
