"And the Lord God commanded the man, saying, 'You may surely eat of every tree of the garden, but of the tree of the knowledge of good and evil you shall not eat, for in the day that you eat of it you shall surely die.'" (Genesis 2:16-17, RSV)

So, God allowed Adam to eat from any tree in the garden except the tree of the knowledge of good and evil.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q: In which of the following ways did God evaluate His work after creation?
explanation: 31 And God saw everything that he had made, and behold, it was very good. And there was evening and there was morning, a sixth day - Genesis 1:31 (RSV)

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q: What was the rationale behind Reuben placing Joseph into a dry well? To
explanation:
The story can be found in Genesis 37:12-36

According to this biblical account, Joseph's brothers were jealous of him because their father, Jacob, showed favouritism towards Joseph. Joseph had also shared dreams with his brothers in which he seemed to have a position of authority over them, which further fueled their resentment.

Reuben, however, was not in favour of harming Joseph. Instead of letting his brothers kill Joseph, Reuben suggested that they throw him into a pit or a dry well, intending to later come back, rescue him and bring him back home. The idea was to prevent Joseph from being killed by his jealous brothers, and Reuben intended to return and bring Joseph safely back to their father.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q: According to Pharaoh's dream, what did the seven gaunt and thin cows that came out of the Nile River do? They
options: ate all the seven ears of grains
ate up the seven sleek and fat cows
explanation: This dream is described in Genesis 41:1-7. In Pharaoh's dream, he saw seven fat and sleek cows emerging from the Nile River, followed by seven gaunt and thin cows. The thin cows then proceeded to devour or eat up the seven fat cows. This dream was interpreted by Joseph, who was known for interpreting dreams. He interpreted this particular dream as a prediction of seven years of abundance in Egypt followed by seven years of famine.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q: Which of the following was not a sign to convince the Israelites that Moses was sent by God?
options: River Nile becoming a pool of blood
explanation:
The sign that is not specifically mentioned in the Bible as a convincing sign to the Israelites that Moses was sent by God is:

D. Flowers growing from Moses' rod.

The other signs (A. Moses' rod becoming a serpent, B. Moses' hand becoming leprous, and C. River Nile becoming a pool of blood) are documented in the Book of Exodus as miraculous events that occurred to validate Moses' divine mission to lead the Israelites out of Egypt.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q: The dominant trait exhibited by the lsraelites following the ill-report of the spies about Canaan was
explanation: The Israelites displayed unfaithfulness following the negative report of the spies about Canaan because they doubted and murmured against God's promises and leadership through Moses. They expressed a lack of trust in God's ability to fulfil His promises to bring them into the promised land, showing a lack of faith and loyalty towards God despite witnessing His previous signs and wonders. This unfaithfulness led them to despair and a desire to return to Egypt, questioning God's plan for them and ultimately disobeying His commands. (Numbers 14)

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q: Moses gave a portion of his authority to Joshua when he was commissioned before the lsraelites so that Joshua would
explanation:
Moses gave a portion of his authority to Joshua when he was commissioned before the Israelites so that Joshua would: B. be obeyed by the Israelites.

This is based on the books of Numbers and Deuteronomy where Moses, at God's command, appointed Joshua as his successor to lead the Israelites into the Promised Land. Moses laid his hands on Joshua, symbolizing the transfer of authority and leadership. This act was to ensure that the people would recognize and obey Joshua as their new leader.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q: Which attribute of Joshua and Caleb distinguished them from the other spies?
options: The confidence they had in themselves
Their knowledge of the land that they spied
Their preparedness to face any challenge
The absolute trust they had in God
explanation: The attribute of Joshua and Caleb that distinguished them from the other spies was D. The absolute trust they had in God. Twelve spies were sent to explore the land of Canaan. Joshua and Caleb were the only two spies who brought back a positive and faith-filled report, expressing their trust in God's ability to give them victory over the challenges in the land. The other spies were filled with fear and lacked the same level of faith and trust in God.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q:
Up! For this is the day in which the Lord has given Sisera into your hands. Does not the Lord go out before
you? This statement was made by
explanation: This statement is found in Judges 4:14. Deborah, the prophetess and judge of Israel, made this declaration to Barak, the military commander, encouraging him to lead the Israelite forces against Sisera, the commander of the Canaanite army.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q: King Ahab became sad and discontent in his palace as a result of
options: his inability to secure Naboth's vineyard.
the deeds of Jezebel towards Naboth
the conspiracy of the nobles against Naboth
the punishment God promised his household.
explanation: King Ahab became sad and discontent in his palace as a result of A. his inability to secure Naboth's vineyard. This event is recorded in the Bible in 1 Kings 21. Ahab desired Naboth's vineyard, but Naboth refused to sell or exchange it. Ahab's discontent and sadness stemmed from his frustration at not being able to acquire the vineyard. This led to the events that unfolded with the involvement of Jezebel and the tragic fate of Naboth.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q: Eli's failure to decisively discipline his children led to all the following except the
options: capture of the Ark of the Covenant.
humiliation of Israel by the Philistines.
explanation: Eli was a high priest and judge in Israel during the time of the judges. His failure to discipline his sons, who were also priests, resulted in their misconduct and ultimately in God's judgment. However, the institution of the monarchy in Israel occurred later, during the time of Samuel, as the people requested a king. Eli's failures did contribute to significant consequences, such as the capture of the Ark of the Covenant and the collapse of his priestly lineage, but it did not directly lead to the institution of the monarchy.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q: According to Samuel, Saul's stubbornness against God is comparable to
explanation:
This is found in 1 Samuel 15:23. Samuel rebukes Saul for not fully obeying God's command to utterly destroy the Amalekites and their possessions. Samuel equates Saul's disobedience and stubbornness to rebellion, likening it to the sin of idolatry:

For rebellion is as the sin of divination,
    and stubbornness is as iniquity and idolatry.
Because you have rejected the word of the Lord,
    he has also rejected you from being king.” (1 Samuel 15:23 - RSV)

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q: Behold, to obey is better than to sacrifice, and to harken than the fat of rams.... It can be inferred from this statement that God
explanation: This statement is from 1 Samuel 15:22. Samuel is rebuking Saul for not fully obeying God's command, emphasizing that God values obedience more than ritualistic sacrifices. It underscores the importance of genuine obedience and a heart turned toward God rather than mere external rituals or offerings.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q:
Why then did you not obey the voice of the Lord? Why did you swoop on the soil and do what was evil in the
sight of the Lord? The offence of the addressee was that he
options: spared Agag and the fatty animal of Amalek
sacrificed to God without Samuel's approval.
explanation:
The offence of the addressee (Saul) was that he: B. spared Agag and the fatty animals of Amalek.

This can be found in 1 Samuel 15. God had commanded Saul to utterly destroy the Amalekites and all their possessions, but Saul spared King Agag and some of the best livestock. This disobedience led to Samuel's rebuke and the subsequent rejection of Saul as king by God.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q: Which of the following personalities accompanied David in his search for Saul in the wilderness of Ziph?
explanation:
In 1 Samuel 26:6, it is mentioned: "Then David said to Ahimelech the Hittite and to Abishai the son of Zeruiah, Joab's brother, 'Who will go down with me into the camp to Saul?' And Abishai said, 'I will go down with you.'"

So, Abishai is the one who accompanied Saul in the search for David in the wilderness of Ziph according to the biblical account.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q: In which of the following ways did David demonstrate his submission to the will of God? He
explanation: David demonstrated his submission to the will of God in the following way: B. spared the life of Saul. This occurred on more than one occasion when David had the opportunity to harm or kill Saul but chose not to because he respected Saul as the anointed king and trusted in God's timing and judgment. (1 Samuel 24 & 26)

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q: The construction of the Temple by Solomon was in fulfilment of God's prophecy to:
explanation:
The prophecy regarding the building of the Temple is found in 2 Samuel 7:12-13 where God speaks to David through the prophet Nathan and promises a lasting dynasty for David's descendants. 

"When your days are fulfilled and you lie down with your fathers, I will raise up your offspring after you, who shall come from your body, and I will establish his kingdom. He shall build a house for my name, and I will establish the throne of his kingdom forever."

This prophecy is fulfilled through Solomon, David's son, who later becomes the king of Israel and constructs the Temple in Jerusalem. The construction of the Temple is considered the realization of God's promise to David, as conveyed through the prophet Nathan.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q: What was Solomon's response when he discovered that his encounter with God was a dream? He
options: offered burnt offering before the ark
settled a dispute between two harlots
organised a feast for his wives
explanation:
When Solomon discovered that his encounter with God was a dream, his response was:

A. offered burnt offering before the ark.

This incident is recorded in the Bible, specifically in 1 Kings 3:15. After Solomon's dream in which God offered him anything he desired, Solomon woke up and realized it was a dream. He then went to Jerusalem and stood before the Ark of the Covenant, offered burnt offerings, and prepared a feast for all his servants. This indicates his reverence for the dream and his commitment to fulfilling what God had spoken to him in the dream.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q: Which of the following was God's reason for which he gave Solomon what he did not ask for in his encounter with him in a dream?
options: Solomon needed more help from God.
God does not give only one thing to people
Solomon was very special to God
God blesses people who have pure heart
explanation: In 1 Kings 3:5-14, when God appeared to Solomon in a dream and offered to give him anything he asked for, Solomon asked for wisdom to govern the people of Israel. God, pleased with Solomon's request, not only granted him wisdom but also gave him wealth, honour, and long life because of the purity of Solomon's heart and his desire for wisdom to govern justly. Therefore, option D reflects how God blesses those with a pure heart.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q: In which of the following ways did Obadiah demonstrate his loyalty to God during the persecution that occurred in his time? He
options: refused to serve the king's idol
hid the prophets of Yahweh in caves
tore down the altars of Baal
explanation: In 1 Kings 18:3-4, Obadiah demonstrated his loyalty to God during the persecution by hiding a hundred prophets of Yahweh in caves and providing them with bread and water. This act of protecting and sustaining the prophets amidst the threat of persecution reflects Obadiah's commitment to God and his refusal to comply with the king's idolatrous practices.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q: The greatest wrath of God on Israel was incurred during the reign of king
explanation: The reign of King Ahab is often associated with significant disobedience and idolatry in Israel which led to God's displeasure and wrath. The prophet Elijah confronted Ahab and his wife Jezebel for their promotion of Baal worship and other sinful practices. A specific incident illustrating God's wrath during Ahab's reign is the confrontation on Mount Carmel, where Elijah challenged the prophets of Baal (1 Kings 18). Ahab's reign is often considered a period of great apostasy in Israel's history.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q: Deborah's response to Barak's request to accompany him to battle against Jabin was that
options: she will enquire from the Lord
the glory will be given to a woman
she was too busy to join him
the wrath of God will visit Barak
explanation: In Judges 4:9, when Barak requested that Deborah accompany him to battle against Jabin, Deborah responded that the glory of the victory would be given to a woman. This prophecy was fulfilled when Jael, another woman, played a crucial role in the defeat of the Canaanite general Sisera.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q: Naaman's request for two mules of Israelite soil after his cure was a result of his belief that
options: there was magic in the soil of lsrael
Israel's soil was superior to Syrian soil
Hebrew God could only be worshipped on Hebrew soil
he would not be able to visit Samaria regularly
explanation: Naaman's request for two mules of Israelite soil after his cure was because of the belief that the God of Israel, who healed him through the prophet Elisha, could only be worshipped on Hebrew soil. In 2 Kings 5, Naaman, a commander in the Syrian army, was cured of leprosy after following Elisha's instructions to wash in the Jordan River seven times. After his healing, Naaman expressed his desire to worship the God of Israel and sought permission to take two mules' worth of Israelite soil back to Syria because he believed that he could only worship God on that specific soil.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q: Which of the following reasons did King Josiah give when he decreed that the workers in charge of the renovation should not render account to anybody? They were
options: from the priestly family of Zadok
explanation:
King Josiah gave the reason that the workers in charge of the renovation should not render an account to anybody because they were sincere in their transactions. This can be found in 2 Kings 22:5-7 (RSV):

"5 and let it be given into the hand of the workmen who have the oversight of the house of the Lord; and let them give it to the workmen who are at the house of the Lord, repairing the house, 6 that is, to the carpenters, and to the builders, and to the masons, as well as for buying timber and quarried stone to repair the house. 7 But no accounting shall be asked from them for the money which is delivered into their hand, for they deal honestly.”

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q: All the following were attributed to Josiah's reform except
options: making the Book of the Law a reference book
reducing the worship of Yahweh to a very low state
restoring Jerusalem to its original pre-eminence.
explanation: Josiah's reform aimed to strengthen the worship of Yahweh and eliminate idolatrous practices. Therefore, reducing the worship of Yahweh to a very low state is not consistent with Josiah's reform. The other options (making the Book of the Law a reference book, abolishing wizards and medium practices, and restoring Jerusalem to its original pre-eminence) are elements of the reforms attributed to King Josiah in the biblical accounts.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q: What was the reason for which the rebuilding of the Temple was opposed by Rehum and his associates?
options: They lacked the spirit of fair play
Their offer to help was rejected
They had no spirit of tolerance
They were obedient to constituted authority.
explanation: The reason for opposing the rebuilding of the Temple by Rehum and his associates was that they believed the city of Jerusalem was rebellious and wicked. They feared that if the city was rebuilt and its walls finished, the Jews would not pay tribute, customs, or tolls, which would impair the royal revenue. Additionally, they mentioned that the city had a history of rebellion and sedition against kings and provinces, leading to its destruction in the past. All of these prove that Rehum and his associates were intolerant to the people of  Israel.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q: The rebuilding of the Temple in Jerusalem became successful as a result of God's influence over King
explanation: The successful rebuilding of the Temple in Jerusalem is attributed to the decree of Cyrus the Great, the Persian king. King Cyrus issued a decree allowing the Jewish exiles to return to their land and rebuild the Temple. This is recorded in Ezra 1:1-4.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q: Which of the following was not a reason for which Daniel suffered blackmail from his peers? He
explanation: Being an exile from Judah was not a reason for which Daniel suffered blackmail from his peers. In the Book of Daniel, Daniel faced opposition and blackmail from his peers because he had an excellent spirit, distinguished himself in his duties, and remained faithful in his devotion to God, including making petitions to his God. The fact that he was an exile from Judah was not a specific reason for the blackmail but rather a part of his background.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q: During the time of Amos, why were the rich said to be above the law? They
options: could secure any verdict they wanted
formulated the rules which were obeyed
explanation: During the time of Amos, it was said that the rich were above the law because they could secure any verdict they wanted. This reflects a situation where the wealthy individuals had significant influence and power which allowed them to manipulate the legal system to their advantage.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q: The basket of summer fruit in Amos' vision signified the
options: end of fruit season in lsrael
next fruit season in the land
kinds of fruit to eat at that time
end of the people of lsrael.
explanation: In Amos' vision, the basket of summer fruit symbolized the impending judgment on the people of Israel. The Hebrew word for "summer fruit" in Amos 8:2 sounds similar to the Hebrew word for "end". The vision conveyed the message that, just as the summer fruit marks the end of the harvest season, so too would the end come for the people of Israel due to their unrighteousness and social injustices.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q: Jesus' acceptance to be baptised by John was meant to
options: prove that he is a saviour
explanation: When Jesus was baptized by John the Baptist, it was to fulfil all righteousness. This act demonstrated Jesus' obedience to God's will and his identification with humanity. Though Jesus was sinless, he participated in the baptism to align with God's plan and set an example for others. This event is recorded in the Gospel accounts, including Matthew 3:13-17.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q: The temptations of Jesus in the wilderness were meant to
options: show that there was temptation after baptism
show that the devil has great power
prepare Jesus adequately for his ministry.
explanation: Jesus' temptations served as a preparatory period for Jesus before he officially began his public ministry. The temptations were a test of his character, resolve, and commitment to fulfilling God's plan.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q: The assertion that if anyone comes to me and does not hate his own father and his own life, he cannot be my disciple, Jesus meant to say that
options: discipleship is about selfishness and being self-centred
material things are irrelevant to the kingdom of God.
explanation:
Jesus' statement about hating one's own father and life is emphasizing the individual commitment required for discipleship and the decision to follow the path leading to salvation. It emphasizes the personal choice and commitment each individual must make in response to the call of Jesus.

The language of "hate" in this context is exaggerated and is meant to communicate the idea of prioritizing Jesus over everything else, including family ties and personal preferences. However, it doesn't mean a literal call to hatred, but instead, it's about putting the path of discipleship and salvation as the most important priority in one's life. It emphasizes the individual's decision to wholeheartedly embrace following Jesus as the primary objective of their life.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q: No one who puts his hand on the plough and looks back is fit for the kingdom of God. What is required of a true disciple according to the above statement?
explanation: The statement "No one who puts his hand on the plough and looks back is fit for the kingdom of God" emphasizes the need for absolute commitment from a true disciple. It conveys the idea that once a person decides to follow Jesus, they should remain focused and committed to the path of discipleship without turning back or being distracted.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q: One lesson we can learn from the story of the unmerciful servant is that we should
options: ignore the plight of our debtors
take our debtors to the appropriate authority for sanctions.
explanation: The lesson we can learn from the story of the unmerciful servant, found in Matthew 18:21-35, is the importance of forgiveness. In the parable, the servant who was forgiven a massive debt by his master refused to show the same mercy to a fellow servant with a much smaller debt. The point of the story is to emphasize the need for believers to be forgiving and merciful to others, just as God has shown mercy to us.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q: Lord, how often shall my brother sin against me, and l forgive him? Jesus' response to the above question meant forgiveness
options: must be done only seven times
takes place where there is a plea
is exercised when offence is repeated severally
does not need to have any limitation
explanation: In response to Peter's question about how often he should forgive his brother who sins against him, Jesus said, "I do not say to you, up to seven times, but up to seventy times seven" (Matthew 18:21-22). This implies that forgiveness should not have a set limit but should be extended generously and without counting. Therefore, option D, "does not need to have any limitation," is the appropriate answer.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q: Jesus' command to his disciples to put away their sword, as those who take the sword will perish by the sword implies that
options: violence should be a last resort
there is a curse on those who use the sword
explanation: The command "Put your sword back into its place. For all who take the sword will perish by the sword" (Matthew 26:52) by Jesus implies that violence begets violence. It suggests that engaging in violence tends to perpetuate a cycle of violence and retaliation. He is trying to warn about the destructive consequences of relying on the sword or violence as a means of resolving conflicts.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q: Certainly, this man also was with him, for he is a Galilean. Immediately after this accusation, all the following happened except the
options: addressee went out and hanged himself
explanation:
The event where the addressee (Peter) went out and hanged himself is not immediately after the accusation mentioned. Instead, it is a later event involving Judas Iscariot, who betrayed Jesus. The correct sequence of events immediately after the accusation is:

  1. The addressee (Peter) denied knowing Jesus (Matthew 26:72).
  2. The accused (Peter) remembered the Lord's prophecy about his denial (Matthew 26:75).
  3. The cock crowed three times, fulfilling Jesus' prediction (Matthew 26:74-75).

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q: Which of the synoptic gospels states that after Jesus was crucified, the earth shook and the rocks split?
explanation: The statement about the earth shaking and the rocks splitting after Jesus was crucified is found in the Gospel of Matthew. Specifically, Matthew 27:51 describes these natural phenomena occurring at the moment of Jesus' death on the cross.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q: Which of these events in the life of Jesus formed the foundation of the Christian faith? His
explanation: The foundation of the Christian faith is primarily based on the resurrection of Jesus Christ. The resurrection is considered the central event that validates the claims of Jesus as the Son of God and the Saviour. The belief in the resurrection is fundamental to Christian theology as it symbolises victory over sin and death and provides hope for eternal life.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q: The ultimate results of Ananias and Sapphira's deaths were that
options: more souls were won for Christ
explanation: The direct result is that members of the church became honest.  Acts 5:11 (RSV) mentions "great fear came upon the whole church". The fear and reverence that the incident instilled in the church community contributed to a sense of order and seriousness about the consequences of dishonesty or hypocrisy within the early Christian fellowship.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q: The following lessons could be learnt from Saul's experience on his way to Damascus except
options: each individual has a specific purpose on earth
any individual can be an asset to God's kingdom
explanation:
From Saul's experience on his way to Damascus (recorded in Acts 9:1-22), the lessons include:

A. Sinners are special before God: Saul, despite his past actions of persecuting Christians, was chosen by God for a specific purpose, demonstrating God's grace and mercy towards sinners.

B. Each individual has a specific purpose on earth: Saul's encounter with Jesus led to his conversion and redirection of purpose. It highlights the idea that every individual has a unique role or purpose in God's plan.

D. Any individual can be an asset to God's kingdom: Saul, who later became the apostle Paul, became a significant asset to God's kingdom after his conversion, preaching and spreading the message of Christianity.

The lesson not learned from Saul's experience is that ignorantly persecuting God is acceptable. Saul's persecution of Christians was not justified, and his encounter with Jesus led to a profound transformation and realization of the error in his actions.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q: What was the reason for Ananias' reluctance to pray over Saul to regain his sight?
options: Saul had not yet repented of his deeds
Saul was not one of the disciples of Christ
He was aware of his persecution of Christians in Damascus
He was not sure Saul was converted already.
explanation: Ananias' reluctance to pray over Saul to regain his sight was rooted in his awareness of Saul's reputation for persecuting Christians in Damascus. Ananias expressed concern about Saul's past actions and was initially hesitant to approach him. This account is found in Acts 9:10-19 in the New Testament.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q: Herod's arrest of Peter after the execution of James was meant to
options: establish Herod as a good and thoughtful King
show his hatred for the disciples of Christ
win the favour and approval of the Jews
hinder the further spread of the gospel
explanation: Herod's persecution of the apostles, including the arrest of Peter and the execution of James, was done to win the favour and approval of the Jews. The motive behind Herod's actions was to appease the Jewish authorities and gain political favour with them.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q: Jesus condemned the tradition 'Corban' because it
options: was a term of abuse in lsrael
meant that one should not swear
they were people laden with iniquity
explanation: The tradition of "Corban" was condemned by Jesus because it contradicted one's obligation to parents. The term "Corban" referred to a practice where someone dedicated their property or resources to God, declaring it as an offering, and thus, exempting themselves from using it for the support of their parents. Jesus criticized this tradition, emphasizing the importance of honouring one's parents and fulfilling one's responsibilities toward them. This is mentioned in Mark 7:9-13 and Matthew 15:3-9.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q: In the sermon on the Mount, who did Jesus say will inherit the earth? He meant the
explanation: In the Sermon on the Mount, Jesus said, "Blessed are the meek, for they shall inherit the earth" (Matthew 5:5, RSV).

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q: According to Luke, Jesus sent the seventy others on missions as
options: lambs in the midst of wolves
preachers of the kingdom of God
explanation: According to Luke 10:3, Jesus sent the seventy others on missions as "lambs in the midst of wolves." This metaphor emphasizes the challenging and potentially dangerous nature of the mission, where they were vulnerable and needed to rely on God's guidance and protection.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q: . "Ah, Lord God! Behold, I do not know how to speak for I am only a youth."
explanation: The statement "Ah, Lord God! Behold, I do not know how to speak, for I am only a youth" is attributed to the prophet Jeremiah. It is found in Jeremiah 1:6 in the Bible.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q: In the creation story, the responsibility to give names to all living creatures was performed by
explanation:
In the creation story, according to the Book of Genesis, the responsibility to give names to all living creatures was performed by Adam. It is written in Genesis 2:19-20:

"Now the Lord God had formed out of the ground all the wild animals and all the birds in the sky. He brought them to the man to see what he would name them; and whatever the man called each living creature, that was its name. So the man gave names to all the livestock, the birds in the sky, and all the wild animals."

Therefore, Adam, the first man, was given the responsibility of naming the animals.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q:
a. Narrate the events that led to the marriage between Moses and Zipporah.

b. Explain two reasons for which people seek asylum.
explanation:
a. The events that led to the marriage between Moses and Zipporah:

Moses was born into a Hebrew family during a time when the Israelites were enslaved In Egypt, Pharaoh, the ruler of Era, had ordered the killing of all Hebrew baby boys to control their population.  To save Moses' life, his mother placed him in a basket and set it adrift on the Nile River. The basket Was discovered by Pharaoh's daughter, who took Moses as her own and raised him in the Egyptian royal court. 

As Moses grew older, he became aware of his Hebrew heritage and felt a deep connection to his people. One day, while witnessing an Egyptian taskmaster mistreating a Hebrew slave, Moses intervened and ended up killing the taskmaster. Fearing the consequences, Moses fled Egypt and found refuge in Midian.

In Midian, Moses came across a well where he saw seven daughters of the priest of Midian, Jethro attempting to water their father's flock. Some shepherds were harassing them and preventing them from accessing the water. Moses came to their aid, driving away the shepherds and helping the women water their flock. Impressed by Moses' chivalry and strength, the daughters returned home and told their father, Jethro, about the incident. Jethro invited Moses to their home and offered him hospitality. Moses accepted and stayed with Jethro, working as a shepherd and helping to tend to Jethro's flock During his time in Midian, Moses developed a close relationship with Jethrơs family.

 Mones'  kindness, righteousness, and his encounter with the burning bush, where he received a divine call from God to free the Israelites from bondage, earned him Jethro's respect and trust. Moses eventually expressed his desire to marry one of Jethro's daughters, Zipporah. Jethro gave his consent and Zipporah became Moses' wife. They had two sons together, Gershom and Eliezer. After his marriage. Moses received his divine mission from God to return to Egypt and confront Pharaoh, demanding the release of the Israelites. With Zipporah and their children, Moses embarked on the journey back to Egypt to fulfil his God-given purpose.

b. The following are reasons for which people seek asylum:

1. Persecution: Many people seek asylum due to persecution based on factors such as their race, religion, nationality, political beliefs, or membership in a partícular social group. Seeking asylum allows them to escape persecution and find refuge in a country where they
can live without fear of harm.

2. War and conflict: Individuals fleeing war-torn regions or areas plagued by armed conflict seek asylum to protect themselves and their families from the perils of war. Seeking asylum provides them with a chance to seek safety and rebuild their lives in a more stable environment.
3. Hunger: People can seek asylum as a result of hunger occasioned by drought or adverse weather conditions. They will try to leave the country for a country where they can have access to food.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q:
a. Recount how the diplomatic relations between Tyre and Israel enhanced the building of the Temple during Solomon's reign.
b. Highlight two benefits of diplomatic relations among nations.
explanation:
a. In the golden era of King Solomon's reign, the land of Israel thrived under his wise and just rule. Recognising the importance of diplomatic relations, Solomon sought to establish a strong alliance with the powerful city-state of Tyre. Their collaboration played a pivotal role in the construction of the grand Temple.

King Solomon shared his divine vision with King Hiram, the ruler of Tyre; a vision to build a magnificent Temple that would honour God and serve as a beacon of faith for generations to come. Impressed by the depth of Solomon's vision, King Hiram pledged his full support. Tyre, renowned for its maritime expertise and thriving trade, possessed abundant resources and skilled craftsmen. King Hiram ordered the felling of the finest cedars from the forests of Lebanon, known for their majestic trees. These tall and sturdy cedars were transported by sea to provide the foundation for the Temple's construction. Additionally, Tyre's skilled craftsmen, led by the renowned Huram-abi, joined forces with the Israelite artisans. Together, they brought Solomon's vision to life, meticulously crafting the intricate architectural elements of the Temple.

The exchange of knowledge and expertise between Tyre and Israel enriched the project, resulting in a structure of unparalleled beauty. The alliance between Tyre and Israel went beyond the construction itself. Trade flourished between the two kingdoms, as goods and services flowed freely. This economic cooperation ensured the availability of necessary resources and financial support for the Temple's progress. Hiram agreed and decided to have his servants cut the woods into rafts and get them for shipping down the Mediterranean
Sea to Lebanon. And when this was done, Solomon was to pay by supplying food for the king's household. 

The Temple became a place of worship and a symbol of divine presence. The story of Tyre and Israeľ's collaboration in the building of the Temple teaches us the power of unity, shared resources, and collective vision.

b. 

  1. Conflict Resolution: Diplomatic relations provide a platform for nations to engage in peaceful dialogue and negotiation. This helps to prevent and resolve conflicts. Through diplomatic channels, countries can address disagreements, find common ground, and work towards mutually acceptable solutions.

  2. Economic Cooperation: Diplomatic relations facilitate economic cooperation between nations. Through diplomatic channels, countries can negotiate trade agreements, establish economic partnerships, and promote investments. This fosters economic growth, creates opportunities for businesses, and enhances the prosperity of nations involved. 

  3. Cultural Exchange and Understanding: Diplomatic relations foster cultural exchange and understanding between nations. Through diplomatic initiatives, countries can promote educational and cultural programs, exchange scholars and artists, and facilitate people-to-people interactions. This enhances cross-cultural understanding, appreciation for diversity, and promotes a more tolerant and interconnected global society.

  4. Collaboration on Global Challenges: Diplomatic relations enable nations to collaborate on addressing global challenges such as climate change, public health crises, terrorism, and poverty. Through international cooperation, countries can pool resources, share expertise, and develop collective strategies to tackle issues that transcend national borders. Diplomatic efforts are essential in building alliances and coordinating responses to global challenges that require unified action.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q:
a. Recount the events that took place in Judah after the death of King Josiah to the first deportation of the Jews.
b. State four causes of civil unrest in a nation.
explanation:
a. After the death of King Josiah, the events in Judah took a tumultuous turn. Josiah was succeeded by his son, Jehoahaz (also known as Shallum), who reigned for only three months before being deposed by Pharaoh Neco II of Egypt. This incident is recorded in 2 Kings 23:30-34 and 2 Chronicles 36:1-4.

Neco installed Jehoahaz's brother, Jehoiakim (also known as Eliakim), as the new king of Judah. Jehoiakim's reign was marked by political instability and his submission to foreign powers.  Jehoiakim did evil in the sight of the Lord, and eventually, Nebuchadnezzar, the king of Babylon, came against Jerusalem and took him captive.

Jehoiachin (also known as Jeconiah or Coniah), the son of Jehoiakim, succeeded his father as king. However, his reign was short-lived, lasting only three months and ten days. Nebuchadnezzar besieged Jerusalem and took Jehoiachin, along with the treasures of the temple, into captivity in Babylon.

Zedekiah, another son of Josiah, was appointed by Nebuchadnezzar as the last king of Judah. Despite being warned by the prophet Jeremiah to submit to Babylon, Zedekiah rebelled against Nebuchadnezzar, leading to a prolonged siege of Jerusalem. The city fell and Zedekiah was captured. Nebuchadnezzar executed Zedekiah's sons before his eyes, and then he blinded Zedekiah and took him to Babylon in chains.

These events ultimately led to the destruction of the First Temple in Jerusalem and the exile of a significant portion of the Jewish population to Babylon. The Babylonian captivity lasted for seventy years, in fulfilment of the prophecy of Jeremiah. This period is a pivotal and sorrowful chapter in the history of the Israelites, as it marked the end of the Kingdom of Judah and the beginning of the Babylonian exile.

 

b.  Civil unrest in a nation can arise from: 

1. Widespread corruption, nepotism, and lack of transparency in governance can lead to public dissatisfaction and unrest.

2. Repressive regimes, denial of political freedoms, and human rights abuses can spark protests and opposition.

3. Disparities in wealth distribution and economic opportunities can create resentment and social tension.

4. Deep-seated ethnic or religious divisions can lead to conflicts and civil unrest.

5. Discrimination based on race, gender, religion, or other factors can contribute to social unrest.

6. Clashes over cultural or national identity can contribute to social discord.

7. Competition for limited resources, such as water or arable land, can lead to conflicts.

8. Lingering historical injustices or conflicts can resurface and contribute to civil unrest.

9. International tensions or interference can have repercussions at the national level.

10.  Overreliance on foreign aid or economic dependence on specific nations may contribute to instability.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q:
a.  "Let us build with you; for we worship your God as you do, and we have been sacrificing to Him ever since...." Narrate the events that followed this request of the Samaritans.
b.  State four causes of tension among neighbours.
explanation:
a. After the Israelites returned from the Babylonian exile with the permission of King Cyrus to rebuild the temple in Jerusalem, they faced opposition from the Samaritans. The Samaritans approached the returning exiles with an offer to participate in the construction of the temple. However, their motives were not entirely sincere, as there was a history of animosity and religious differences between the Jews and the Samaritans.

The leaders of the returning Jewish exiles, including Zerubbabel and Jeshua, declined the Samaritans' offer to participate in the construction of the temple. They stated that they would build the temple on their own. The Samaritans did not take this rejection well. They became adversaries of the Jews and actively sought to undermine the rebuilding efforts. They engaged in efforts to frustrate and discourage the Jewish community, both through political means and by spreading false accusations against the Jews.  

During the reign of Artaxerxes (successor to Ahasuerus), Rehum, a high official, and his associates wrote a letter to the king, expressing concern about the reconstruction activities in Jerusalem. The letter accused the Jews of planning to rebel against the king once the city and walls were rebuilt. The letter also cited the historical rebelliousness of Jerusalem and its potential threat to the Persian Empire.

In response to Rehum's letter, Artaxerxes issued a decree to halt the construction of the city and the temple in Jerusalem. This decree ordered a thorough investigation into the matter and instructed that the building activities cease until further notice. It was not until the reign of King Darius of Persia that the construction received official support again. The prophets Haggai and Zechariah played significant roles in encouraging the people to resume the construction, which eventually led to the completion of the temple. 

The events in Ezra highlight the challenges and opposition faced by the returning Jewish exiles as they sought to rebuild the temple in Jerusalem. The Samaritans' initial offer to join in the construction turned out to be insincere, leading to a period of conflict and interruption in the rebuilding process.

 

b. The following are four possible causes of tension among neighbours:

1. Religious and Cultural Differences: Different beliefs, practices, and historical animosities can create tensions among neighbours. The rejection by the Jewish leaders of the Samaritans' offer to participate in the temple construction could be rooted in religious and cultural differences.

2. Historical Grievances and Mistrust: Past conflicts or perceived injustices can linger and contribute to ongoing tensions between neighbouring communities. The Samaritans' opposition to the Jews may have been influenced by historical grievances and mistrust.

3. Competition for Resources: Competition for resources, whether economic or symbolic, can lead to tensions among neighbours. The Samaritans' desire to join in the construction of the temple could have been driven by a strategic interest in sharing the benefits or resources associated with the temple.

4. Political and Power Dynamics: Political ambitions, power struggles, and the fear of potential rebellion can create tension among neighboring regions, especially when there is a perceived threat to the ruling authority. The involvement of high officials like Rehum and his associates in writing to Artaxerxes suggests a political dimension to the tensions.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q:
a. Then Jesus said to him, begone Satan! For it is written, you shall worship the Lord your God and Him only shall you serve. Narrate the events that took place before Jesus made the above statement as recorded in Matthew.

b. Highlight three lessons which Christians can learn from Jesus' encounter with Satan in (a).
explanation:
a. According to Matthew 4:1-11, after His baptism by John the Baptist, Jesus was led by the Spirit into the wilderness to be tempted by the devil. This period of fasting and testing lasted for forty days and forty nights. The devil approached Jesus and tempted Him to turn stones into bread because of His hunger after the extended fast. Jesus responded by quoting Scripture, saying, "Man shall not live by bread alone, but by every word that proceeds out of the mouth of God".

Then, the devil took Jesus to the pinnacle of the temple in Jerusalem, tempted Him to throw Himself down and suggested that the angels would rescue Him. Jesus responded by quoting Scripture, saying, "Again, it is written, 'You shall not put the Lord, your God, to the test'"

After this, the devil took Jesus to a high mountain and showed Him all the kingdoms of the world and offered them if Jesus would bow down and worship him. In response, Jesus said, "Begone, Satan! For it is written, 'You shall worship the Lord your God and him only shall you serve" (Matthew 4:10). Following these temptations, the devil left, and angels came to minister to Jesus 

These events depict a significant moment in Jesus' life. In each instance, Jesus relied on the authority of Scripture to respond to the temptations and assert His commitment to worship and serve God alone. 

 

b. 

Here are three key lessons from Jesus's temptations:

1. The Power of Scripture in Spiritual Warfare: Jesus consistently responded to each of Satan's temptations by quoting Scripture. This shows the importance of knowing and relying on God's Word as a powerful weapon in spiritual warfare. Christians can learn that a deep understanding of the Scriptures helps in resisting temptation, discerning truth from deception, and standing firm in faith.

2. Resisting Temptation through Faithfulness to God: Jesus' unwavering commitment to God is evident in His responses to Satan. He refused to compromise His allegiance to God, even when faced with enticing offers. Christians can learn the importance of staying faithful to God in the face of temptation, trusting in His promises, and understanding that true fulfilment comes from obedience to God's will.

3. The Importance of Worship and Service to God Alone: Jesus' final response to Satan emphasized the exclusive worship and service due to God. Christians can learn the significance of prioritizing God in their lives, ensuring that their worship is directed only towards Him. This lesson encourages believers to resist the allure of worldly power, recognition, or material possessions that may divert their devotion from God.

4. The Reality of Spiritual Warfare: Jesus' confrontation with Satan portrays the reality of spiritual warfare. Christians can learn that they are engaged in a spiritual battle against the forces of darkness. This lesson emphasizes the importance of being vigilant, putting on the full armour of God, and recognizing that the enemy seeks to tempt, deceive, and undermine one's faith. Christians are called to be spiritually prepared and equipped to face and resist the schemes of the adversary.

5. Dependence on God's Strength: Throughout the temptations, Jesus relied on the strength and guidance of the Holy Spirit. Christians can learn the importance of dependence on God's strength in times of trial. Instead of relying on their own abilities, believers are encouraged to seek the empowerment of the Holy Spirit and recognise that victory over temptation and spiritual challenges comes through the grace and strength provided by God.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q:
a. Lord, how often shall my brother sin against me, and I forgive him? Narrate Jesus's response to the above question.

b. Outline three benefits one stands to gain in forgiving others.
explanation:
a. Matthew 18:21-22 (RSV) states: "Then Peter came up and said to him, 'Lord, how often will my brother sin against me, and I forgive him? As many as seven times?' Jesus said to him, 'I do not say to you seven times, but seventy-seven times.'"

In response to Peter's question about forgiveness, Jesus emphasizes the importance of limitless forgiveness. The number "seventy-seven times" is symbolic, indicating that forgiveness should not be limited to a specific number but should be extended generously and without bounds. 

Jesus began to tell a parable to illustrate His point about forgiveness. "Therefore, the kingdom of heaven may be compared to a king who wished to settle accounts with his servants." The king began the settlement, and one servant was brought before him who owed an immense amount—an astronomical sum that he could never hope to repay.

As he could not pay, his master ordered him to be sold, with his wife and children and all that he had, and payment to be made." The servant, in desperation, pleaded for mercy, falling on his knees and saying, 'Have patience with me, and I will pay you everything. Out of pity for him, the master of that servant released him and forgave him the debt. The king showed extraordinary and unmerited mercy by cancelling the entire debt of the servant.

However, this parable took a turn.  When that same servant went out, he found one of his fellow servants who owed him a hundred denarii." Compared to the colossal debt he had been forgiven, this was a comparatively small amount. Yet, the forgiven servant seized his fellow servant and began to choke him, demanding immediate repayment. Hearing of this, his fellow servants were greatly distressed and reported the matter to the master. The master summoned the unforgiving servant and said, 'I forgave you all that debt because you pleaded with me. And should not you have had mercy on your fellow servant, as I had mercy on you?'"

In the end, the master handed the unforgiving servant over to the jailers until he could pay all his debt. Jesus concluded the parable with a powerful statement, "So also my heavenly Father will do to every one of you if you do not forgive your brother from your heart."

Through this parable, Jesus conveyed the crucial message that since God has forgiven us of an immeasurable debt of sin, we should likewise extend forgiveness to others, no matter how small their offences may seem in comparison. The parable highlights the importance of a merciful and forgiving heart in response to God's abundant forgiveness.

 

b. The following are the benefits one stands to gain by forgiving others:

1. Receiving God's Forgiveness: Forgiveness opens the door for individuals to receive God's forgiveness for their own shortcomings and sins. The forgiven servant in the Parable of the Unforgiving Servant experienced an extraordinary act of mercy from his master, who cancelled a massive debt. When individuals forgive others, they align themselves with the principles of mercy and forgiveness that God exemplifies. As you forgive, you also receive forgiveness.

2. Freedom from Bitterness and Unhealthy Emotions: Forgiveness is liberating. It allows individuals to experience emotional healing and freedom from the negative emotions that can accompany unresolved conflicts. The Parable of the Unforgiving Servant highlights the destructive consequences of an unforgiving heart through the actions of the servant who, though forgiven generously, refused to extend the same mercy to a fellow servant. When individuals choose to forgive, they release themselves from the burdens of bitterness, anger, and resentment.

3. Building Healthy Relationships:  Forgiveness is a key component in building and maintaining healthy relationships. When individuals choose to forgive, it fosters reconciliation and restores harmony in relationships. Forgiveness promotes a culture of understanding, empathy, and love. In the parable, the unforgiving servant's actions strained his relationship with his fellow servant and led to negative consequences.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q:
a.  Outline the circumstances that led to the untimely death of Ananias and Sapphira.

b. State four lessons the church of today can learn from the actions of Ananias and Sapphira that led to their death.
explanation:
a. The following is an outline of the circumstances that led to the deaths of Ananias and Sapphira:

1. In the early Christian community in Jerusalem, believers were known for their generosity and willingness to share their possessions. Acts 4:32-37 describes how members of the community sold their properties and brought the proceeds to the apostles to be distributed to those in need.

2. Ananias and Sapphira, a married couple, also sold a piece of property but chose to keep back a portion of the proceeds for themselves. They conspired together to present only a part of the money to the apostles while giving the impression that it was the full amount (Acts 5:1-2).

3.  Ananias presented the money to the apostles, falsely claiming that it was the entire proceeds from the sale of their property. The apostle Peter, however, confronted Ananias, questioning why he had allowed Satan to fill his heart, leading him to lie not to men but to God (Acts 5:3-4).

4. Upon hearing Peter's words, Ananias fell down and died instantly. The fear of God came upon all who heard about it, and young men came, wrapped up his body, and carried him out for burial (Acts 5:5-6).

5. After about three hours, Sapphira entered the scene, unaware of what had happened to her husband. Peter questioned her about the price of the land, and she confirmed the amount her husband had given. Peter then exposed her deception, saying that she had conspired with her husband to test the Spirit of the Lord. Sapphira also fell down dead, and she was buried next to her husband (Acts 5:7-10).

6. The sudden deaths of Ananias and Sapphira had a profound impact on the early Christian community. Great fear came upon the whole church and all who heard about these events. The incident served as a powerful warning about the seriousness of deceit and hypocrisy within the community of believers (Acts 5:11).

7. The circumstances leading to the deaths of Ananias and Sapphira highlight the importance of integrity, sincerity, and honesty within the early Christian community, portray the severe consequences of attempting to deceive the Holy Spirit and the consequences of hypocrisy.

 

b. The account of Ananias and Sapphira in the early Christian community, as recorded in Acts 5, provides several lessons that the church today can learn. The following are some of them:

1. Importance of Honesty and Transparency: Ananias and Sapphira's downfall began with their deceitful actions. The church today can learn the critical importance of honesty and transparency in all matters, whether financial, relational, or spiritual. Maintaining integrity fosters trust and authenticity within the community.

2. Sincerity in Generosity: The early Christian community was characterized by a spirit of generosity. Ananias and Sapphira's mistake was not in keeping some of the money but in pretending to be as generous as others who gave all. The church today should emphasize sincerity in acts of kindness, charity, and financial contributions and avoid hypocrisy.

3. Understanding the Holiness of God: Ananias and Sapphira's deception was not just a lie to people but, as Peter pointed out, a lie to the Holy Spirit. The church today should grasp the holiness of God and the seriousness of living transparently before Him. This understanding should influence individual and corporate conduct.

4. Fear of God and Reverence: The sudden and severe consequence of Ananias and Sapphira's actions instilled a fear of God within the early church. The contemporary church can learn the importance of maintaining a healthy fear of God, acknowledging His holiness, and living in awe of His presence.

5. Accountability within the Community: the apostolic response to the deceit of Ananias and Sapphira highlights the principle of accountability within the Christian community. The church today can learn the importance of leaders and fellow believers holding one another accountable. This will foster an environment of mutual correction and support.

6. Grace and Repentance: While the story of Ananias and Sapphira is a solemn one, it also emphasizes the gravity of sin and the importance of repentance. The church today can learn to appreciate God's grace and the opportunity for repentance by turning away from dishonesty and embracing a path of righteousness.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q:
a. Outline James' instructions to Christians on how to conduct effective and result-driven prayer.

b. State four factors that hinder prayer from being heard and answered.
explanation:
a. The instructions on effective and result-driven prayer from the Book of James in the New Testament provide practical insights for Christians. Here is an outline of James' teachings on prayer. James begins by emphasizing the need for wisdom when facing trials. He encourages believers to ask God for wisdom with faith, believing that God will generously provide. However, he warns against doubting, as a double-minded person should not expect to receive anything from the Lord.

James also addresses the issue of unanswered prayers by pointing out that sometimes prayers go unanswered because people ask with wrong motives. He emphasizes the importance of aligning one's desires with God's will, rather than seeking selfish pleasures. Asking in accordance with God's will is essential for effective prayer. 

James addresses the practice of calling for the elders of the church to pray over the sick. He encourages believers to pray for one another, confess their sins and seek healing. James also explains the effectiveness of fervent, righteous prayers and cites Elijah as an example of a man whose prayers brought about significant results. Elijah prayed earnestly for rain. Persistent, heartfelt prayer produces powerful results.

James concludes his letter by addressing the importance of praying for those who have strayed from the truth. He encourages believers to actively seek the restoration of those who have wandered. He recognises the significance of intercessory prayer in bringing back wayward individuals.

 

b. According to James' teachings on prayer, several factors can hinder prayers from being heard and answered. The following are some of them:

1. Doubt and Double-mindedness (James 1:6-8): Lacking a firm and unwavering belief in God's ability to answer prayer can hinder the effectiveness of prayer. A person who doubts is like a wave of the sea, tossed by the wind, and such a person should not expect to receive anything from the Lord.  James emphasizes the importance of faith in prayer.

2. Selfish Motives (James 4:3):  Praying with selfish motives hinders the reception of God's favourable response. James points out that prayers can go unanswered when people ask with wrong motives and seek to fulfil selfish desires. Prayer, according to James, is not a tool for self-indulgence but an opportunity to align one's desires with God's will.

3. Unconfessed Sin (James 5:16a): In the context of praying for healing, James encourages believers to confess their sins to one another. Unconfessed sin can create a barrier to communication with God. To experience effective prayer, there is a need for a genuine acknowledgement and confession of sins. This helps to ensure a clear and open relationship with God.

4. Lack of Righteousness and Fervency (James 5:16b-18): James emphasizes the effectiveness of the prayers of a righteous person. Lack of righteousness and fervency in prayer can hinder its efficacy. Genuine and fervent prayers, exemplified by the persistent and passionate prayer of Elijah, are more likely to produce powerful results. A lack of righteousness and fervency may impede the impact of prayer.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q:
(a) Highlight Peter's advice to elders and the youth of the church on how to promote good relationships.
(b) Indicate three ways by which conflict retards the growth of the church.
explanation:
Peter provides advice to both elders and young people in the church in his first epistle (1 Peter). Peter advises elders to shepherd the flock of God among them. This involves providing guidance, care, and protection to the members of the community  Elders are encouraged to serve willingly and eagerly, not out of compulsion or for personal gain. The emphasis is on a genuine desire to serve the community with a servant's heart (1 Peter. Elders are also urged to be examples to the flock. This involves living lives worthy of emulation by modelling the Christian virtues they preach and demonstrating the principles of faith and love. Peter stresses the importance of humility and instructs elders to clothe themselves with humility in their interactions with one another and with the congregation. Humility fosters unity and positive relationships. Elders are also encouraged to cast their anxieties on God, trusting in His care and provision. This advice acknowledges the challenges and responsibilities that come with leadership and emphasizes reliance on God's support (1 Peter 5:7).

Peter advises young people to submit to the authority of elders. This submission is rooted in a recognition of the role and responsibility of those in leadership positions within the community. Similar to the advice given to elders, Peter encourages young people to clothe themselves with humility. This emphasizes the importance of a humble disposition in relationships which fosters mutual respect and cooperation. Young people are advised to be sober-minded and watchful in spite of spiritual challenges they may face. This involves being vigilant in their faith and resisting the temptations that may come their way. Peter also advises young people to resist the devil steadfastly in their faith. This underscores the spiritual warfare aspect of the Christian life and the importance of standing firm against negative influences.

 

b.  The following are ways that conflicts can potentially retard the growth of the church. 

1. Conflict within the church can lead to division and a breakdown of unity among the members. When conflicts arise, and there is a lack of resolution or reconciliation, it can undermine the fellowship and cooperation needed for the church's growth. A divided church is less effective in carrying out its mission and impacting the community positively.

2. Conflicts within the church can tarnish its reputation and diminish its positive Christian witness to the community. External perceptions of disunity and conflict can hinder the growth of the church by limiting its outreach and impact.

3. A church characterized by internal strife and discord may struggle to attract new members and effectively share the message of the Gospel.

4. Conflicts can divert the attention and energy of church leaders and members away from the core mission of preaching the Gospel, making disciples, and serving the community. Managing internal conflicts demands time and resources that might otherwise be directed toward the growth and outreach efforts of the church.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

explanation: B: Genesis 1:6-8: "And God said, Let there be a firmament in the midstof the waters, and let it divide the waters from the waters, And God made the firmament, and divided the waters which were under firmament from the waters which above the firmament: and it was so. And God called the firmament Heaven. And the evening and the morning were the second day."

q: The determination of times and seasons was made possible with the creation of ?
explanation: God created light for the determination of times and seasons. Genesis 1:14 -16. Genesis 1:14 - 19 provides the explanation; "and God said let there be light in the firmament of the heaven to divide the day from the night; and let them be for signs and for seasons and for days and years.

q: Which of the following was man's responsibility to other creatures?
explanation:
B. Genesis 1: 26 - 28, "and God said, Let us make man in our image, after our likeness: and let them have dominion over the fish of the sea, and
over the fowl of the air, and over the cattle, and over all the earth, and over every creeping thing that creepeth upon the earth."

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q: In which of the following ways did God prepare Joseph for his leadership role in Egypt? Through the
options: love his brothers had for him
explanation: B: God prepared Joseph for his leadership role in Egypt through the trials and tribulations he endured in his early life. Genesis 37-50 counts forJoseph's story, where he faces numerous challenges, including being sold into slavery by his jealous brothers, being falsely accused and imprisoned,and enduring years of separation from his family. Despite these hardships, Joseph remained faithful to God and continued to trust in His plan for his life. Through these trials Joseph developed important qualities such as resilience, humility, wisdom, and reliance on God. These experiences also provided him with valuable leadership skills, aqs he learnt to interpret dreams, manage resources,and govern effectively during his time in Egypt.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q: Which of the following was the greatest reason for which Joseph's brothers decided to do away with him?
options: his father made him a special robe
he was loved most by their father
explanation: D: The correct answer is the meaning of his dreams. Gemesis 37:3-8 provides insight into the reason why Josephs brothers haboured resentment towards him. Joseph's dreams further exacerbated the situation as they depicted him ruling over his brothers which angered them even more (Genesis 37:5-11). It was clearly stated in Genesis 37: 20 that Joseph's dreams of superiority contributed to their decision to plot against him and eventually sell him into slavery.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q: The twelve men Moses sent to spy out the land of Canaan were meant to verify the following except the
options: strength and weakness of the inhabitants
population and size of the land
size of the army and their weaponery
topography and fertility of the land
explanation: C: The correct answer to the question is size of the army and their weaponry. When Moses the twelve spies to explore the land of Canaan, their main objectives were to assess the strength and weaknesses of the inhabitants, the population and size of the land, and the topography and fertility of the land. This is detailed in Numbers 13:17-20.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q: the presence of God among His people during the day on their way out of Egypt to the wilderness of Sinai was felt in the form of
explanation:
C: During the Isrealites' journey from Egypt to the wilderness of Sinai, God's presence among them was manifested in the form of a cloud during the day and a pillar of fire at night. Exodus
13:21-22.

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q: Which of the following was God's condition to Joshua if he wishes to be successful? He should
options: meditate on the book of the law
explanation: C: God's condition for Joshua's success is clearly outlined in Joshua 1:8: "Do not let this Book of the Law depart from your mouth; meditate on it day and night, so that you may be careful to do everything written in it. Then you will be prosperous and successful."

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
q: When the people of Israel heard the majority report of the spies, they took a decision to
options: pray to God to come to their aid
choose a leader and return to egypt
stone joshua and caleb for blasphemy
explanation:
C: The correct answer is choose a leader and return to Egypt. After hearing the majority report of the
spies, the Israelites responded with fear and unbelief. Numbers 14:1- 4 describes their reaction.
